title: FDA Retail Food Regulatory Program Standards Self-Assessment and Verification Audit Workshop
info: The Standards Self-Assessment and Verification Audit Workshop is designed to provide participants with an overview of the Retail Program Standards criteria and an in-depth understanding of the self-assessment and verification audit processes, worksheets, and forms. The two-and-a-half-day workshop will enable participants to: Identify the requirements for each of the nine Retail Food Program Standards; Explore various approaches to meeting the criteria within the Retail Food Program Standards; Use the Retail Food Program Standards worksheets and forms to complete a verification audit of a jurisdiction’s self-assessment against the criteria contained in the standards; and Use the worksheets and forms to electronically compile and maintain results. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED Please note, this SAVA Workshop is only available to state, local, tribal, and territorial retail food regulatory programs.

The two-and-a-half-day workshop will enable participants to: Identify the requirements for each of the nine Retail Food Program Standards; Explore various approaches to meeting the criteria within the Retail Food Program Standards; Use the Retail Food Program Standards worksheets and forms to complete a verification audit of a jurisdiction’s self-assessment against the criteria contained in the standards; and Use the worksheets and forms to electronically compile and maintain results. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: Environmental Health Photo Exhibition
info: The Environmental Public Health Photography Contest returns for its fourth year at the 2026 AEC, showcasing the power of imagery to tell the story of environmental health. Under this year’s theme, EH in View – Seeing What Matters, the exhibit highlights compelling visuals from our members that capture the challenges, impacts, and everyday work of the environmental health profession. From fieldwork moments to community impacts, each photograph invites viewers to see the issues that shape environmental health. The exhibit will be on display throughout the AEC for all attendees to experience and enjoy. Join us Tuesday evening at 5:00 pm, for the announcement of our contest winners!

title: Registration
info: Stop by the Registration Desk to check in to the conference and pick up your badge. Then, visit the Info Table to ask questions about the conference, get directions, collect badge ribbons, and obtain information on the various programs and services offered by NEHA.

title: Aquatic Venue Public Health Inspector Course
info: Gain valuable insight and knowledge on the in's and out's of inspecting public pools with this CDC Model Aquatic Health Code (MAHC) based training from CMAHC. Attendees who successfully complete the course will gain a 5-year certification as an Aquatic Venue Public Health Inspector. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: Calm in the Chaos: Practical De-Escalation Skills for Environmental Health Professionals
info: Environmental health professionals often find themselves on the front lines of conflict, navigating tense situations with the public, colleagues, or stakeholders. This interactive four-hour workshop offers practical tools to stay grounded, communicate effectively, and defuse difficult encounters before they escalate. Join Alicia Love and Dr. Lucy Houghton for a dynamic, hands-on learning experience that blends real-world scenarios, group discussions, and reflective practice. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: CDC BRACE Framework: Building Resilience Against Climate Effects
info: NEHA, in collaboration with experts from ecoAmerica, will host a four-hour pre-conference workshop on the updated Building Resilience Against Climate Effects (BRACE) framework at the 2026 NEHA AEC in Kansas City, Missouri. This interactive session will build the workforce’s capacity to assess climate and health threats, engage communities, and develop evidence-based strategies that strengthen climate and health resilience. Designed for environmental and public health professionals, the workshop will provide practical tools, real-world examples, and collaborative approaches to protect families and communities from climate-related health risks. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: FDA Retail Food Regulatory Program Standards Self-Assessment and Verification Audit Workshop
info: The Standards Self-Assessment and Verification Audit Workshop is designed to provide participants with an overview of the Retail Program Standards criteria and an in-depth understanding of the self-assessment and verification audit processes, worksheets, and forms. The two-and-a-half-day workshop will enable participants to: Identify the requirements for each of the nine Retail Food Program Standards; Explore various approaches to meeting the criteria within the Retail Food Program Standards; Use the Retail Food Program Standards worksheets and forms to complete a verification audit of a jurisdiction’s self-assessment against the criteria contained in the standards; and Use the worksheets and forms to electronically compile and maintain results. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: Know the Signs, Stop the Spread: Practical Pests Control with Purpose
info: Rodents, cockroaches, and flies remain persistent public health threats. This interactive workshop is designed for professionals in code enforcement, food safety, housing, community health, and vector control. Participants will learn how to identify signs of infestations, assess environmental conditions, and apply effective Integrated Pest Management (IPM) strategies—focusing on prevention, sanitation, exclusion, and education. The training includes classroom learning, group activities, and a field tour to build practical skills for sustainable pest control and community engagement. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: Private Water Network’s, Well Under Pressure: Advancing Competencies to Empower Private Well Professionals
info: Over 43 million Americans rely on private wells—often without regulatory oversight or standardized safety protocols. This workshop addresses the urgent need for technical training and field-based skills to protect public health in communities served by private water systems. Designed for professionals at all experience levels, this workshop offers expert-led sessions that will equip private water professionals with the tools and confidence to evaluate and identify safe water infrastructure, assess well systems, monitor water quality, and respond to contamination risks effectively. Participants will gain practical skills, scientific insight, and communication strategies to guide homeowners toward informed decisions. Join us to enhance your knowledge, strengthen your skills, and build your capacity to protect your community’s drinking water. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: Climate Ambassador Training
info: This interactive training equips environmental health professionals with the knowledge and tools to lead local climate and health discussions. Through hands-on activities and expert insights, participants will learn how to communicate climate impacts, promote health equity and strengthen community resilience. Emphasizing the power of local leadership, this training empowers professionals to be effective change agents in driving climate solutions. Upon completion, participants will be eligible to become a Climate for Health Ambassador. PRECONFERENCE WORKSHOP REGISTRATION REQUIRED

title: Managing Water Well: Water Management for Food Manufacturers, Home Food Producers, and Produce Farms
info: Food entrepreneurs, manufacturers, home or cottage kitchens, produce farms, and other members of the food industry are required to use high quality water to produce food. Many of these entities located in rural areas rely on well water, which may be impacted by an array of factors, including the overall condition of the aquifer, surrounding land and water features, and the construction of the well itself. This short course (4 hours) is intended to teach these members of the food industry how to ensure the quality of their well water is adequate to use for food production and sanitation. This short course discusses well management, water testing and treatment, and septic systems and how they may impact water quality.

title: Opening Keynote Address
info: Dr. Michael Osterholm, Director of the Center for Infectious Disease Research and Policy (CIDRAP) at the University of Minnesota, will deliver the Opening Keynote Address, offering timely insights on the evolving public health landscape and the critical challenges shaping our future preparedness and response.

info: Food Safety at the World Cup

title: Advancing Environmental Resiliency for the Future of Tennessee
info: Despite having no dedicated staff or funding to address environmental resiliency, the Tennessee Department of Health formed an internal working group to address increasing environmental hazards in the state. Since 2024, the group has worked with two national organizations on climate and health, integrated environmental health topics across existing Departmental priorities, focused on external partnerships, and increased access to training for staff. his presentation will describe how staff have furthered work to increase both Departmental and population health resiliency despite challenges related to funding, staff capacity, and language.

title: Foodborne Illness Escape Room
info: Not Just Fun and Games: Using an Escape Room to Increase Knowledge of Foodborne Illness Investigations Like puzzles? Enjoy public health detective work? Feeling competitive? Come test your skills and see if you can beat the escape room developed by Ferris State University and the Kent County Health Department to teach people about foodborne illness investigation. Sign up is located at

title: From Practice to Research: Lessons from Implementing Air Quality Projects in Alaska Native Communities
info: Alaska Native communities in the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta face high rates of respiratory disease, driven in part by poor indoor air quality in remote cold-climate homes. This session presents findings from the Clean Air Study, an intervention examining HEPA purifier effectiveness in high-particulate Alaska Native households. Learn how a multi-disciplinary team of tribal health and uniformed services professionals designed and deployed a rigorous field study under challenging conditions and what the results mean for environmental health practice. Practical lessons for implementing low-cost air quality interventions in remote and underserved communities will be highlighted.

title: Got Green Air? An evaluation of 14 low-cost sensors for indoor air quality monitoring
info: As climate change intensifies the frequency and severity of wildfires, communities face growing risks from wildfire smoke, particularly through its impact on indoor air quality (IAQ). Few studies have evaluated the effectiveness of low-cost IAQ sensors for household-level monitoring and decision-making. Public Health-Seattle and King County in collaboration with the University of Washington CEER Lab have conducted a project evaluating 14 low-cost air quality sensors commonly cited in scientific literature and consumer reports. This evaluation used laboratory-based performance testing to identify the precision and accuracy of a variety of low-cost sensors and assessed facilitators and barriers to the overall accessibility of these sensors for the general public. These findings will help inform the development of guidance on household-level IAQ monitoring and support strategies to reduce the health impacts of air pollution in wildfire-prone regions.This learning lab will provide participants an opportunity for hands-on engagement with various low-cost IAQ sensors.

title: High Stakes Pools: Don’t Gamble with Public Health in Southern Nevada
info: What happens in Vegas... gets inspected. Behind the glamour of the Las Vegas Strip lies thousands of aquatic venues where inspectors encounter some of the most unique and challenging pool environments in the country. From mega-resorts with unique ideas such as in-water hookah services, day clubs, and surf simulators to unregulated cold plunges, aquatic venues in Southern Nevada push the boundaries of traditional regulations. The Southern Nevada Health District (SNHD) inspects aquatic health facilities in Clark County, Nevada, overseeing more than 5000 permits year-round. These facilities include resort pools, water parks, HOA pools, apartment complexes, health spas, and unpermitted venues. Through real-world scenarios, attendees will learn how SNHD inspectors apply risk-based enforcement and implement communication strategies in their daily inspections. Discover how SNHD Aquatic Health Inspectors adapt, innovate, and respond daily to protect public health while building trust with operators, managers, and the public in Southern Nevada.

title: Improving Outbreak Investigation and Prevention: Understanding Contributing Factors and Environmental Antecedents
info: Analyzing foodborne outbreak data is crucial for identifying trends, developing effective control measures, and preventing future outbreaks, as it provides valuable insights into how and why outbreaks occur. CDC’s National Environmental Assessment Reporting System (NEARS) collects crucial data on contributing factors and environmental antecedents that explain how and why outbreaks happen. The first part will provide a background and introduction of these concepts and their adaptability to different outbreak types. The second part will review recent data from two publications, highlighting common themes in foodborne illness outbreaks. Finally, attendees will learn about resources designed to assist inspectors and investigators, including online learning labs, short videos, and a guide on handwashing violations. By integrating research findings with practical tools, this presentation aims to equip participants with actionable insights to enhance food safety practices and effectively combat foodborne illnesses.

title: Indigenous Food Taboos and Food Safety among the Krobo of Ghana: A Community-Based Qualitative Study
info: “The bigger the gutter, the sweeter the Waakye.” This Ghanaian saying captures a vivid tension between cherished taste beliefs and food safety realities. Our community-based, qualitative study in four Krobo communities examines how indigenous knowledge—proverbs, taboos, and ancestral obligations—influences everyday food choices, especially for pregnant women and children. Using purposive and snowball sampling, we conducted semi-structured interviews and focus groups, audio-recorded, transcribed, translated, and thematically analyzed. We found snails are widely prohibited, with compliance motivated by reverence for ancestors, promised blessings, and fear of sanctions. At the same time, participants cited crab and lobster as nutritional substitutes during pregnancy, yet the overall food safety and nutrition literacy remained low. By surfacing when indigenous rules protect health and when they inadvertently create risk, this research invites collaboration between public health, nutrition, and cultural leaders. We propose pathways to respectfully engage local knowledge while strengthening evidence-based food safety for rural communities.

title: Marking Memory: From Indigenous Traditions to Cremains in Modern Tattooing
info: Tattooing is more than skin deep—it’s a powerful expression of culture, memory, and identity. From the ancient Indigenous hand poke and skin stitching techniques that embody land, ceremony, and resilience, to the contemporary practice of embedding cremated remains into tattoo ink as a living memorial, tattooing traditions are evolving yet deeply rooted in meaning. This presentation explores the intersections of these practices with environmental public health, offering insights into respectful regulation, cultural safety, and health equity. Join us to discover how honoring ancestral methods and modern memorial customs can inform safer, culturally affirming tattoo practices

title: The Future of Environmental Health: NEHA's Student Research Competition Winners Present
info: Join us as we spotlight the winners of the 2026 Student Research Competition! This session features two outstanding student researchers—one undergraduate and one graduate—presenting their award-winning projects that tackle today’s most pressing environmental health challenges. Learn from the next generation of leaders and explore how our initiatives are supporting academic excellence and workforce development. Don’t miss this opportunity to be inspired by fresh ideas, innovative research, and rising talent in the field.

title: Understanding Body Art in Environmental Health: Current Status, Emerging Trends, and Program Impacts
info: Body art is changing fast! Are you keeping up? Explore current trends, program goals, and emerging issues. Learn how partnerships with organizations, like the Association of Professional Piercers (APP) help EH programs make a real impact. Perfect for EH directors and professionals interested in protecting public health. Attendees will leave with actionable insights to guide policy, inspections, and education strategies in their jurisdictions.

title: You Are Not an Inspector, You Are an Educator: Transforming Food Safety Through Communication
info: Cut out the fear factor and learn how to transform inspections and corporate visits into opportunities for learning. This session blends storytelling, instructional design, and interactive tools to help you build trust, reduce fear, and improve training outcomes, whether you're working in regulation or industry.

title: Exhibition Hall
info: Join fellow professionals and industry leaders for an engaging opportunity to explore the newest products and services shaping the future of environmental health! Connect, learn, and enhance your expertise in a casual networking atmosphere.

title: Coffee Break
info: Join us for coffee in the Exhibit Hall

title: CDC Partnerships Addressing Harmful Algal Blooms at Public Beaches
info: Freshwater beaches are enjoyed by millions, but they can pose risks. Exposure to HAB toxins can pose a serious threat to human health. CDC funded five jurisdictions to develop strategies to prevent HABs exposures at inland freshwater beaches used for recreation. Through monitoring systems and public communication strategies, grantees kept the public informed when HABs risks were present.

title: Clean Air: A Moral Imperative and Communication Tool for Climate Action
info: Americans are increasingly aligned on the importance of clean air for the health of our families and communities. Recent polling from ecoAmerica showed that 92% say clean air and water are critical rights for all people, including 97% Democrats, 90% of Republicans, and 91% of Independents. It is clear from this data that there is bipartisan support for improving air quality. From the direct burning of fossil fuels to changing weather patterns to worsening wildfire smoke and increasingly severe allergy seasons, climate change and air quality are inextricably connected. By engaging with climate initiatives targeting air quality, we can protect our health now and address air pollution in the future. This session will discuss full spectrum advocacy and action on climate change and air quality.

title: Climbing the Ladder Without Falling Off - Advancing Women in EH (A NEHA LeadHERship session)
info: Transitioning into supervisory and leadership roles in environmental health can be exciting but challenging, especially for women in a traditionally male-dominated field. This interactive session explores strategies to advance your career while maintaining credibility, balance, and impact. You will learn how education, professional credentials, and involvement in environmental health organizations can boost expertise and visibility. Networking, mentorship, committees, and community engagement are highlighted as practical tools for professional growth. Through interactive exercises and real-world examples shared by fellow attendees, together we’ll develop skills for team management, decision-making, and confident communication, while identifying actionable strategies to achieve your leadership goals. By the end of the session, you will leave with practical tools, resources, and inspiration to pursue leadership opportunities and advance your career in environmental health with confidence.

title: Control of Communicable Diseases on Interstate Conveyances
info: Discover how FDA's Interstate Travel Program protects millions of travelers from communicable disease threats on buses, trains, aircraft, and vessels. This presentation explores the evolution of federal sanitation standards from 19th-century cholera outbreaks to modern COVID-19 responses. Learn about the regulatory framework under 21 CFR Parts 1240 & 1250, common pathogens like norovirus and Legionella encountered on conveyances, and FDA's coordinated outbreak response protocols. Attendees will gain insights into multi-layered mitigation strategies, inspection authorities, and enforcement mechanisms that balance public health protection with interstate commerce. Essential knowledge for public health professionals working at the intersection of transportation safety and infectious disease control.

title: Legionella pneumophila in Cooling Towers: Progress Advancing Public Health Regulation
info: For the first time, cooling tower biocides can officially claim to target Legionella pneumophila—thanks to a new EPA-approved pathway that puts public health front and center.

title: More Than a Mandate: Growing Leaders, Building Culture, and Delivering Health in a Brand-New Department
info: Join leaders from the Delaware County, PA Environmental Health Department for an invigorating discussion on forging a modern, resilient public health team. Established just four years ago, this department faced the unique challenge of building its entire workforce and leadership structure from the ground up during a post-COVID-19 era. Learn their proven playbook for cultivating a culture where employee development isn't an HR task—it's the core strategy. This panel will reveal how they designed leadership pipelines, balanced technical expertise with essential transferable skills, and successfully trained and retained a diverse workforce. Whether you're starting a new initiative or revitalizing an established one, gain practical, strategic insights to analyze your own workforce opportunities and formulate a robust framework for a sustainable future. Don't just manage your team—master the art of growing your future leaders.

title: Onsite Wastewater System Dilemmas: Beyond One-Size-Fits-All Approaches
info: Discover how geographic factors influence onsite wastewater system selection and long-term performance across the United States. NEHA's Private Water and Decentralized Wastewater Program Committee will examine prominent technologies used in different regions, explaining why proper system-to-site matching is crucial for public health protection. Learn about system benefits, typical service life expectations, and common challenges that compromise performance. Explore root causes of system failures and understand why comprehensive inspection, testing, and maintenance programs are essential. Gain tools for better system evaluation, problem identification, and stakeholder education that will strengthen your community's decentralized wastewater management program.

title: Retail Standards Assessment Tool
info: NEHA’s new Retail Standards Assessment (RSA) tool streamlines the completion of the Self-Assessment of All Nine Standards (SA9) and Comprehensive Self-Improvement Plan (CSIP) by providing a standardized, cloud-based platform modeled after FDA’s Gap Analysis spreadsheet. This interactive learning lab will introduce jurisdictions to the tool, highlight its benefits for the 2026-2027 RFFM Grant Year, and allow attendees to practice using it firsthand—be sure to bring your laptop or tablet.

title: Roaches, Rodents, and Rats, Oh My!
info: This season, join food safety and pest control professionals as they tackle the evolving challenges of building and managing a holistic pest control program. With pest pressures shifting and regulations tightening, discover what businesses are doing to stay ahead of infestations while safeguarding food safety. You’ll hear from industry experts about innovative strategies for pest prevention, cutting-edge tools, and creative approaches to managing common (and not-so-common) pest issues. And yes—we’re keeping it fun! Get hands-on with interactive pest games, including the unforgettable “Guess the pest” challenge, and explore practical tools to help you evaluate and strengthen your own pest prevention program.

title: Society of Permanent Cosmetic Professionals: Providing Permanent Makeup Safety, Excellence, and Professional Standards
info: As a consumer or regulator, have you seen the sub-standard tattoo work that has been propagated on women through large, trendy brow tattooing? Have you seen the increase in salons offering the microblading service? Have you seen an increase in complaints to your regulators’ office of technicians that have done irreparable and costly harm to a women's esthetics? The Society of Permanent Cosmetic Professionals is a non-profit organization that seeks to help young technicians get on the right path to success in this industry. This presentation will seek to educate you on the cosmetic tattoo industry, safety procedures, things the regulators should look for and some steps that can be taken to assist the entrepreneur opening their new permanent cosmetic studio business.

title: Weaving a Stronger Tapestry: The Development of an Environmental Health Competency Model for the Indian Health Service
info: A new environmental health competency model designed to empower IHS and Tribal staff is being rolled out by the Indian Health Service. This presentation reveals the multi-stage process behind this vital framework. Attendees will learn how the new model and its self-assessment tool will be used to manage careers, guide professional development, and identify crucial training opportunities, ultimately building a more skilled and resilient workforce.

title: Co-Creation of Story-Based User Guides for a Climate Resilience Tool: An Alaska Case Study
info: This short lecture will cover the co-production methods employed to create tailored public health messaging through visual storytelling for audiences in Alaska to encourage health-protecting strategies during wildfire smoke events. The resulting comics will be shared as well as recommendations for similar future efforts.

title: Dangerous Lettuce? A Closer Look at Foods Identified During Foodborne Illness Outbreaks in Restaurants
info: Every year, an estimated 9 million people in the United States become ill due to foodborne illness; many foodborne illness outbreaks originate in restaurants. To prevent such outbreaks, it is helpful to know three things: which pathogens most often cause outbreaks, which foods most often transmit those pathogens, and which environmental factors most often lead to outbreaks. In this session, attendees will learn about the most common foods identified during outbreak investigations in restaurants, along with associated pathogens and contributing factors. Understanding interactions between foods, pathogens, and contributing factors can help public health authorities and restaurants formulate protocols and practices that help prevent foodborne illness outbreaks.

title: Modernizing Public Health: Leveraging Technology to Scale Colorado’s PFAS Testing and Assistance Program (PFAS TAP)
info: From one rural zip code to 640 statewide, Colorado’s PFAS Testing and Assistance Program (PFAS TAP) shows how technology and automation can streamline program delivery while keeping the work community-centered. Attend the session to peek behind the curtains and learn the strategies that modernized private well testing programs.

title: NEHA Update: Region 8 Vice President’s Perspectives and Review
info: Overview of selected NEHA activities and trends with specific focus on how they actually or potentially impact Uniformed Services members.

title: Reimagining Environmental Surveillance: Using Artificial Intelligence to Predict and Prevent Emerging Contaminant Risks
info: Discover how artificial intelligence (AI) is transforming environmental health surveillance and decision-making. This session explores real-world data applications that use AI to detect and predict contamination from PFAS, microplastics, and VOCs before they reach critical levels. Attendees will learn how AI models improve accuracy, streamline inspections, and strengthen early warning systems, offering a proactive approach to managing environmental risks and building resilient, data-driven public health programs.

title: Using Cooling Tower Registries to Improve Legionnaires' Disease Surveillance and Outbreak Response
info: The difference between life and death during an outbreak is often related to how fast public health can identify source(s) of exposure and initiating mitigation efforts. This session will review the positive impact of cooling tower registries on surveillance, outbreak response, and initiating mitigation. The session will end with discussion for how to create highly accurate cooling tower registries without the need to adopt formal regulations.

title: Lunch Break
info: Lunch Break

title: A Community Participatory Science Research Collaborative - Microbial Source Tracking of Bacterial Contamination in Recreational and Aquaculture Waters
info: In collaboration with the ESDHD and a Neighborhood Civic Association, this project set-out to fulfill several water quality project aims. These broadly include updating previous reports on bacterial contamination, volunteers carrying out water sampling tests, mapping local sewage systems, administering a sanitary survey, and collaborating with local/state partners. Our local public health department worked to improve water quality and sustain community relationships within this neighborhood, and through this citizen science project we learned valuable lessons in community engagement and recognized our local community members for their volunteer service.

title: CDC and NACCHO’s MAHC Network - 12 Years of Partnership
info: Comprehensive and up-to-date pool codes can help community officials ensure that public aquatic venues remain safe and healthy environments for the public to enjoy. To better support Local Health Departments (LHDs) maintain safe public pools to optimize prevention of disease, drowning, and other injuries, the National Association of County and City Health Officials (NACCHO) launched the MAHC Network in 2014 with support from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). Based on CDC’s Model Aquatic Health Code (MAHC) this online platform provides technical assistance, tools, and resources to LHDs who safeguard public swimming venues.

title: Detection, Reduction, and Destruction: NSF’s Varied Approaches to Tackling PFAS
info: Discover how NSF’s standards, testing, and certification programs are responding to PFAS risks. This session highlights efforts to safeguard drinking water, including through the use of household treatment systems. Additionally, to break the cycle of “forever chemicals”, a standard is being developed for validating the effectiveness of technologies that destroy PFAS in spent water treatment media. Lastly, programs have been developed to protect food from harmful contamination as well. PFAS-free claims for various food contact products, which are solutions that manufacturers can pursue and regulators can demand, will also be discussed.

title: Freedom to Choose, Duty to Protect: Rethinking Food Freedom
info: Food freedom sits at the intersection of culture, science, and consumer choice, sparking passionate debate across communities. Supporters view it as a matter of autonomy, tradition, and access to minimally processed foods. Critics emphasize the risks of foodborne illness, the necessity of regulation, and the duty to protect public health. This session will create a respectful, moderated forum where both perspectives can be explored with clarity and balance. Expert panelists will present evidence ranging from nutritional claims and consumer rights to epidemiological data and outbreak investigations. Together, they will examine questions such as: How should regulators weigh personal freedom against safety obligations? What role do local economies and cultural identity play? Can innovative strategies—like transparent labeling or herd health programs—offer middle ground? Designed as a dialogue, not a debate, the session emphasizes fact-based discussion, civil exchange, and opportunities for shared understanding.

title: From Classroom to Kitchen: Building Food Safety Inspectors for Success
info: The Portage County Health District (PCHD) offers a unique onboarding program that prepares new food inspectors for success by combining education, mentorship, and hands-on experience. Grounded in the FDA Voluntary Retail Program Standard 2: Trained Regulatory Staff, the program ensures inspectors gain both technical skills and professional confidence. New hires begin with structured classroom instruction and are paired with experienced mentors who guide them through observation, supervised inspections, and independent fieldwork. Trainees also use digital tools, including electronic inspection reports and GIS mapping via PCHD’s Mapping the Menu initiative, to simulate real-world scenarios and enhance learning. By blending structured training, supportive mentorship, and technology, PCHD transforms new staff into competent, confident food safety professionals. From classroom to kitchen, this program exemplifies a modern, engaging approach to building the next generation of food safety leaders.

title: From Mosquitoes to Machine Learning: When AI Meets Environmental Health
info: Artificial Intelligence may not replace environmental health professionals anytime soon—but it is ready to be the intern who never sleeps. This interactive session explores what happens when algorithms join the public health team (no hard hat required). Participants will learn the basics of prompt engineering that work across AI platforms and can be applied to everyday public health challenges—from generating data insights and predictions to identifying the genus of mosquitoes (for now!) before your second cup of coffee kicks in. AI works fast—sometimes impressively fast, sometimes confidently wrong. Through hands-on activities and real-world examples, participants will see how AI can boost efficiency, spark creativity, and occasionally make you double-check everything. By the end of the session, you’ll understand why AI makes a great personal assistant for environmental health practice—just do not trust it to take field notes, complete inspection reports, or survive a site visit without close supervision.

title: Public Health Implications of Harmful Cyanobacteria in Washington's Large River Systems
info: The proliferation of toxin-producing cyanobacteria in some of Washington’s Columbia and Snake Rivers presents a unique and challenging public health concern. Both rivers are deeply culturally and ecologically significant to the welfare of regional Tribes, economies, and food webs. Therefore, properly characterizing the complexity of this environmental health problem, developing on-the-ground response strategies, and fostering interagency partnerships are all key to ensuring the public is well-informed and protected. Attendees will hear from state and local governments in Washington about all of the above as well as recommendations for future work along these large river systems.

title: Sanitization for Food Safety: Using Sanitizer Test Strips
info: Think you know all there is to know about test strips? Think again! Test strips are essential for verifying sanitizer strength—but small mistakes in storage, use, or interpretation can throw results off and put health at risk. This session unpacks those nuances, showing how to match the right strip to the right sanitizer, read results accurately, and avoid common errors. After reviewing the basics of cleaning vs. sanitizing and the most common retail sanitizers, you’ll put your skills to the test in a hands-on workshop. Walk away confident in your ability to use test strips correctly and ensure safer practices in the field.

title: Targeted Environmental Health Interventions: Lessons from Sioux Falls’ Project NICE and KEEP
info: Sioux Falls, South Dakota, has transformed neighborhood cleanup into a data-driven, community-powered initiative through Project NICE and Project KEEP. By pairing GIS mapping with hands-on support, the city directs cleanup resources where they are needed most—removing hundreds of tons of debris, engaging volunteers, and keeping nuisance cases steady despite rapid population growth. This model shows how cities can turn environmental challenges into opportunities for healthier, stronger neighborhoods.

title: The Southern Environmental Health Study: Design and Findings From an Exposome-Wide Study for Cancer Risk Research
info: This session will describe considerations in the design and implementation of a new exposome-wide study, the Southern Environmental Health Study (SEHS). This large prospective cohort study is being conducted to address gaps in research evaluating environmental exposures and cancer risk in humans. Preliminary findings from the study, including biomarkers and passive wristband samplers, will also be shared including how they might inform cancer risk research.

title: Woven Through One Health: Inspiring the Next Generation of Environmental Health Professionals
info: Discover how One Health can shape the next generation of environmental health professionals. This session highlights a collaborative strategy led by the University of Kentucky College of Public Health, Lexington-Fayette County Health Department, Franklin County Health Department, UK Cooperative Extension, in collaboration with other community partners to increase student exposure to environmental health careers. Using DEAL Day as one example, participants will explore how hands-on, immersive experiences connect students to real-world practice while strengthening the workforce pipeline. Attendees will learn how partnerships between academia, local health departments, and community organizations can create meaningful career pathways, enhance agency visibility, and inspire students to explore environmental health. Through evaluation insights and lessons learned, this session demonstrates practical ways to engage future professionals, weave together education and practice, and build a more resilient, interconnected workforce. Join us to see how strategic collaboration can strengthen the fabric of environmental health through One Health.

title: Advancing Equity and Safety for Private Well Users Through Education, Policy, and Partnership
info: Private well users in Minnesota face limited protections against drinking water contaminants after their wells are constructed. Beyond the initial water test required by the Minnesota Well Code, responsibility for monitoring and treatment falls entirely on the well owner. To address these gaps, the Minnesota Department of Health (MDH) has leveraged CDC Environmental Health Capacity funding to develop educational materials that raise awareness and reduce risks. Efforts have focused on integrating well water information into real estate transactions and working to ensure rental property tenants have access to safe drinking water. Building on six years of work, MDH is now seeing these strategies extended by local partners and advocates. This presentation will highlight lessons learned, policy proposals related to private wells, and new initiatives emerging from the extended fifth year of CDC EHC funding—demonstrating how partnerships and policy can strengthen protections for private well users.

title: And what do you do? Exploring the public's understanding of Environmental Health
info: What do people really know about Environmental Health and the work carried out by Environmental Health Practitioners? Based on research which looks at the public's knowledge, perceptions and awareness of EHPs, this session considers if we are an invisible profession (where the more effective we are the more invisible we become) and what can be done to try and increase the visibility and viability of the profession.

title: Coordinated Community Action: The Lincoln-Lancaster County Heat Response Plan
info: In response to rising temperatures and increasing heat-related illnesses, the Lincoln-Lancaster County Health Department (LLCHD) in Lincoln, Nebraska launched a community-based Heat Response Plan in May 2025. Developed in collaboration with over 40 community partners, the plan aims to reduce the health impacts of extreme heat through coordinated action, resource distribution, and public education. Key strategies include the activation of cooling centers, distribution of heat relief kits, and multilingual outreach through the “Stay Safe in Extreme Heat” campaign. A Heat Vulnerability Index was used to prioritize high-risk areas, while an Extreme Heat Tabletop Exercise helped assess community preparedness. With a focus on protecting vulnerable populations this plan represents a scalable, community-driven model for local climate resilience and public health response. The session will explore lessons learned, tools used, and how cross-sector collaboration is vital to tackling the growing threat of extreme heat.

title: Educating Missouri Communities to Thrive in a Changing Climate
info: Bridging local Missouri action research with national dialogue to empower communities and environmental health professionals - including sanitarians, nurses, and engineers - against climate threats.

title: Environmental Health and Sanitation Support to the United States Disciplinary Barracks and the Joint Regional Correctional Facility, Fort Leavenworth, KS
info: How do you apply core environmental health principles within the unique operational constraints of a military maximum-security prison? This presentation details the two-part framework used at Fort Leavenworth to safeguard a diverse community, including the Department of War's only maximum-security prison. We will explore how a standard installation-wide program is adapted to meet the heightened disease transmission risks of a correctional environment and how the Defense Occupational and Environmental Health Readiness System (DOEHRs) is leveraged for robust surveillance and cost-benefit analysis. We are able to use this model for proactive environmental health management in any high-risk, resource-limited setting.

title: Legionnaires’ Disease in the US: National Trends, Primary Prevention, and Environmental Health Resources
info: Environmental Health plays a key role in the prevention of Legionnaires’ Disease. This presentation will refresh the audience on the basics of disease causes and spread. It will also briefly touch on recent CDC literature and surveillance related to Legionella. The presentation will then outline the key environmental health elements of Legionella outbreak response and prevention with highlights of how CDC assists local jurisdictions and builds capacity. Prevention resources will be outlined with an opportunity for the audience to engage on topics such as environmental testing and sampling, water management programs, prevention activities, and water system basics.

title: Smoke Ready Spokane: Use of a Community Needs Assessment to Inform Climate Resilience Planning
info: Wildfire season has grown in length and severity in the Inland Northwest due to decades of fire suppression and climate change. While many regions in the US have experienced improved air quality in recent years, the Inland Northwest has seen worsening air quality and associated public health impacts, as a result of increased wildfire activity. In response, the University of Washington and Gonzaga University conducted a Community Assessment for Public Health Emergency Response (CASPER) in the City of Spokane to assess household perceptions of wildfire smoke and other environmental hazards and their health impacts; household strategies to reduce exposure; and preparedness needs. The results will be used to inform community climate resilience preparedness efforts, including a citywide smoke plan and a network of community resilience hubs. This presentation will cover the CASPER methodology, including survey development, volunteer recruitment, and data analysis, as well as survey findings.

title: The Emerging Threat of Microplastics in Water Systems: Implications for Environmental and Human Health
info: Microplastics are now among the most widespread environmental contaminants, impacting water quality, aquatic life, and human health. This session explores global research on how microplastics move through water systems, the toxicological risks they pose, and innovative strategies to reduce their spread. Attendees will gain insight into standardized monitoring, policy reforms, and sustainable interventions that can protect communities and ecosystems from this emerging global threat.

title: Coffee Break
info: Join us for coffee in the Exhibit Hall

title: Are You Really Listening? Strategies for Effective Communication
info: Environmental public health professionals work at the critical intersection of technical scientific expertise and human connection. This session introduces environmental health professionals to the power of active listening in everyday practice. Through interactive exercises and scenarios, participants will learn how listening with intention can reduce conflict, strengthen trust, and enhance the impact of public health programs. Whether responding to community concerns, conducting inspections, dealing with staff or collaborating with partners, effective listening is essential for advancing environmental public health goals.

title: Before and After MAHC Adoption: Strategies and Lessons Learned with adopting a new aquatics code
info: Adopting a new aquatic health code can be difficult and daunting for an environmental health department but it can be done with the right amount of determination and working proactively with your communities. Establishing lines of communication and having the right supporting documents can be influential in not only the implementation of the code but getting the direct community 'buy in' to get aquatic venues into compliance. Learn from a county health department that had successfully adopted the MAHC and the success and challenges during this process.

title: Building the Future of Environmental Health: How Sudbury Is Leveraging Massachusetts Training Hubs to Standardize and Strengthen the Local EH Workforce
info: The Sudbury Health Department is redefining environmental health (EH) workforce development through the Massachusetts Training Hubs initiative. In partnership with the Eastern Massachusetts Public Health Training Hub, Sudbury is using standardized, competency-based training to rapidly onboard and professionalize new EH staff. The Training Hubs are addressing long-standing challenges of fragmented training and inconsistent onboarding across local health departments. By integrating the Training Hub's tiered courses, online modules, and structured mentorship into its onboarding system, Sudbury has built a scalable, equitable model that enhances staff confidence, retention, and readiness. Early outcomes demonstrate improved efficiency and stronger regional collaboration, showcasing how even small local departments can leverage statewide infrastructure to strengthen environmental health capacity and community protection across Massachusetts.

title: Cyber-Security: A Growing Threat
info: Cyber attacks are on the rise. Environmental public health agencies, especially water and wastewater agencies, have been targeted and ransoms paid. Local governments face cyber attacks threats daily. Public water and wastewater agencies have seen a dramatic rise in attacks, with vulnerabilities such as outdated default passwords, unsecured Human-Machine Interface (HMI) devices, and weak overall cybersecurity standards. Attacks can disrupt operations or steal data. This session will invite experts from FEMA and other programs to discuss with the audience:- how to prepare local environmental public health agencies for cyber attacks- how to prevent cyber attacks from threatening local environmental health agencies, and- how to respond if an attack is successful Local environmental public health agencies should be prepared for an attack on their systems. This session will help them prepare, prevent, and respond to any threats from a cyber attack.

title: Environmental Health Perspective of Pool and Splashpad Conditions Leading to Naegleria fowleri Infection
info: Environmental Investigation of Naegleria fowleri in Recreational Water

title: Into the Wild: Environmental Health on a Grand Canyon Backcountry River Expedition
info: Experience public health in the wild! Join me on a multi-day Grand Canyon backcountry river expedition, where environmental health work takes place miles from roads or infrastructure. From inspecting food setups, verifying drinking water and wastewater systems, and monitoring sanitation at multiple camps, to promoting disease prevention and rabies awareness, this session explores public health in its most remote context. Learn how adaptability, creativity, and field logistics are essential when serving populations in rugged wilderness areas. This session offers a rare glimpse into environmental health practice beyond the office, perfect for those curious about backcountry, high-stakes public health work.

title: Meeting Local Environmental Health Needs: A Training Needs Assessment and Beyond
info: The Wisconsin Environmental Health Capacity program will share its process for identifying the training needs of Wisconsin's local environmental health workforce. We will describe the process used to develop the assessment, lessons learned along the way, key findings, and how we are implementing new resources to meet these needs.

title: Regulations, Inspections & the Tattoo Trade
info: This session helps connect body art regulation to real-world practice and fosters valuable collaboration between health officials and practitioners.

title: Stopping the Spread - Legionnaires' Disease Investigation in Southern Nevada
info: What happens when a hotel is making people sick in a global tourism destination? This case study will walk you through the process of an outbreak investigation at a large-scale resort in Las Vegas. Learn about how the Southern Nevada Health District responds proactively to single cases of Legionnaire’s Disease and how we elevate our response for an outbreak. This session will cover water management program review, exposure site identification, water and swab sampling, decision-making, relationship-building, and recommendations for contaminated water systems.

title: Using the BRACE Framework for Climate & Health Actions
info: Local public health action on climate change is more important than ever. The Building Resilience Against Climate Effects (BRACE) Framework was recently updated. The updated BRACE Framework includes an accompanying Implementation Guide and aims to empower health professionals in advancing health equity and climate resilience. A key priority in this update and revision was further integrating and centering justice, equity, and belonging. This session will take a deep dive into the key elements of the updated BRACE Framework, highlighting a wealth of resources and worksheets included in the accompanying Implementation Guide, and featuring real examples from the field of health professionals using the BRACE model. To further support health officials in using the BRACE framework the “BRACE: A Public Health Model for Climate Action” training was recently launched. Join this session to learn how you can use the BRACE Framework to elevate your public health climate actions.

title: What’s in a Definition? The FDA’s Universal Approach to Reevaluating the Food Code’s Definition of Time/Temperature for Safety (TCS) Foods
info: Keeping foods cold under proper temperature control can prevent foodborne illness, but which foods require refrigeration? Join this collaborative learning lab where FDA will present its comprehensive approach to reevaluating the Food Code's Time/Temperature Control for Safety (TCS) food definition. Industry operators, regulators, and academia are invited to share pain points, solutions, and practical insights as FDA undertakes a holistic review of TCS foods. Through dialogue and interactive discussions, attendees will examine real-world application issues, economic and operational impacts, and identify practical tools to meet operational challenges when applying the TCS food definition. This session will be facilitated and held in a workshop format that will provide a unique opportunity for industry, regulatory, and academic stakeholders to influence FDA's approach to revisit the TCS food definition while addressing regulatory clarity, emerging food technologies, and enhanced public health protection through evidence-based policy development.

title: Environmental Health Capacity Cooperative Agreement: Strategies to Identify and Prioritize Hazards and Assess the Effectiveness of Interventions
info: Before we can improve environmental health (EH) practice through the identification, adoption, and implementation of evidence-based EH best practices, we need to identify and prioritize EH hazards, address hazards through interventions, and show the impact and effectiveness of EH interventions. The Environmental Health Capacity (EHC) Cooperative Agreement was designed to improve EH practice support improvement in identifying and prioritizing EH hazards, and impact and effectiveness of EH interventions. Attend this session to find out what EH hazards were identified and prioritized, how they were addressed, and the impact of EH interventions used to address them. You may be inspired to adopt similar strategies and interventions for your service area.

title: Assessing the Association Between Nitrate in Water and Neural Tube Defects in Nebraska
info: Nitrate contamination in drinking water is a growing concern across the Midwest, where agricultural activity contributes to high levels of groundwater nitrate concentrations. Nebraska faces elevated risks due to its widespread reliance on private wells and the use of fertilizers. In this study, we examined the relationship between nitrate exposure in groundwater and NTDs, along with other birth defects in Nebraska. By integrating nitrate and natality data, we examined how maternal intake of nitrate-contaminated water may influence risks for infants, as well as creating a multivariable logistic regression model to identify other factors associated with the selected birth defects.

title: Faces of Environmental Health: A Demographic Reflection of the EH Workforce
info: Environmental health professionals serve diverse communities, but the workforce itself doesn’t always reflect that diversity. This session uses PH WINS data from 2021 and 2024 to examine demographic shifts in race, age, tenure, and education. Participants will explore how these trends impact equity, leadership, and the future of environmental health.

title: From the Ground Up: Surveying the Needs of the Rural and Frontier EPH Workforce
info: What does the rural and frontier environmental public health workforce need most to succeed? The National Environmental Health Association (NEHA) and its Rural and Frontier Program Committee (RFPC) sought to address this question through a national survey. This session presents key findings—directly from professionals serving on the frontlines—regarding resources, tools, and professional development. Join us to explore how these insights can drive stronger support and lasting impact for communities nationwide.

title: NCEH Safe Water Activities: Federally Unregulated Water Systems and Emerging Contaminant Research
info: Millions of Americans rely on water sources that fall outside federal regulation—private wells, building and small water systems, and recreational waters—leaving critical gaps in protection. The CDC Safe Water Team is addressing these challenges head-on through innovative science, strategic partnerships, and real-world technical assistance. From Legionella prevention and well contaminant risk communication to microplastics research and emergency response, this session will explore how environmental health practitioners can leverage national tools, data, and guidance to protect their communities. Join us to learn how your work connects to a broader strategy for safer water and healthier environments.

title: Protecting a Critical Source Water: Groundwater Regulation and Monitoring in Shelby County, Tennessee
info: Shelby County, Tennessee, home to Memphis, relies entirely on groundwater for its drinking water source. As the only county in the state with its own groundwater authority, the Shelby County Health department plays a critical role in protecting this resource through well construction standards, permitting, inspections, and abandonment protocols. Its Water Well Program oversees more than 700 active wells and conducts county wide water sampling to detect contaminants. This presentation explores the regulatory strategies, field operations, and proactive monitoring that safeguards the aquifer for future generations.

title: Uniformed Services Environmental Health Association Business Meeting
info: Brief overview of USEHA present activites and future endeavors.

title: Valley Fever (Coccidioides): Expansion, Climate Proliferation, Infrastructure Vectors, and Future Epidemic Thresholds
info: Valley Fever, or coccidioidomycosis, is reshaping its endemic boundaries under the combined pressures of climate-driven aridification and expanding anthropogenic presence. This session will layer several environmental models and epidemiological trends to map the growing spread of Coccidioides spp. from 2025 to 2050, noting the springboarding role of rail and highway corridors influencing spore dispersal. We will also examine predictive incidence surges, crossing epidemic thresholds by 2040, and correlate expanding geographic risk with projected healthcare system strain and socioeconomic impact. By integrating climate projections, soil ecology, and transportation network analysis, participants will gain a modular risk framework with aims for proactive surveillance and mitigation. This discussion strives to broaden public health practitioner tools thus laying groundwork for adaptive infrastructure planning, targeted occupational controls, and cross-sector coalition strategies to anticipate and respond to Valley Fever’s evolving threat and community resilience building strategies.

title: Student Excellence Reception & Career Fair
info: Open to all - students and professionals at every stage. While we celebrate accomplishments of the next generation of environmental health professionals, we also encourage experienced professionals to attend, connect, and share insights. There will be dedicated time to engage with employers and explore opportunities at the career fair. Come professionally dressed as complimentary professional headshots will also be provided. Be sure to stop by the HS GovTech table to learn about exciting opportuinites in tech.

title: Environmental Health Photo Exhibition & Contest
info: The Environmental Public Health Photography Contest returns for its fourth year at the 2026 AEC, showcasing the power of imagery to tell the story of environmental health. Under this year’s theme, EH in View – Seeing What Matters, the exhibit highlights compelling visuals from our members that capture the challenges, impacts, and everyday work of the environmental health profession. From fieldwork moments to community impacts, each photograph invites viewers to see the issues that shape environmental health. Winners will be announced at this time, and the exhibit will be on display throughout the AEC for all attendees to experience and enjoy.

title: Career Connections Lounge
info: Your space to network, explore careers, and make meaningful connections. What you'll find: ✔ A job posting bulletin board – find or share open roles ✔ A quiet meeting space for career conversations ✔ Opportunities for informal interviews and networking How It Works: Post a job? Pin it to the board. Seeking a job? Check the board & connect through the app. Need a quiet spot to talk? Come on in. Use the conference app to schedule chats with hiring representatives. Open all conference long!

title: Environmental Health Photo Exhibition
info: The Environmental Public Health Photography Contest returns for its fourth year at the 2026 AEC, showcasing the power of imagery to tell the story of environmental health. Under this year’s theme, EH in View – Seeing What Matters, the exhibit highlights compelling visuals from our members that capture the challenges, impacts, and everyday work of the environmental health profession. From fieldwork moments to community impacts, each photograph invites viewers to see the issues that shape environmental health. The exhibit will be on display throughout the AEC for all attendees to experience and enjoy. Join us Tuesday evening at 5:00 pm, for the announcement of our contest winners!

title: Registration
info: Stop by the Registration Desk to check in to the conference and pick up your badge. Then, visit the Info Table to ask questions about the conference, get directions, collect badge ribbons, and obtain information on the various programs and services offered by NEHA.

title: Wednesday Plenary Address
info: The State of Smoke: Review of Established and Evolving Evidence on the Health Effects of Air Pollution from Landscape Fires

title: Anticipatory Action for Mass Gatherings: A Framework for Advancing Environmental Health Surveillance
info: Mass gatherings present unique environmental health challenges, from infectious disease outbreaks to chemical exposures and misinformation. Traditional public health surveillance systems, centered on clinical and laboratory notifications, often result in delayed responses, limiting the effectiveness of containment efforts. This session introduces the E-SAFE framework (Environmental Health Surveillance and AI for Event Protection), a novel, anticipatory surveillance model that integrates artificial intelligence, wastewater monitoring, and human-in-the-loop analytics to detect and respond to threats before they escalate. Attendees will explore how multi-layered data, from emergency departments, retail indicators, and social media, could be synthesized to guide proactive public health actions. Designed for scalability and sustainability, E-SAFE offers a blueprint for transforming traditional surveillance systems into dynamic predictive tools. Learn how this approach can enhance mass gathering readiness while strengthening environmental health infrastructure and community resilience.

title: Climate Change, Health, and Food
info: Find out what 16% of people may be willing to change for the sake of the environment and their health.

title: Digital Waves: How Social Media is Changing Environmental Health Surveillance
info: What if the next public health risk was hiding on your “For You” page? Social media is at the forefront of the 21st century; social media has become one of the most influential tools in shaping public behavior - and now it is helping environmental health specialists at the Southern Nevada Health District to identify risks. Through real examples discovered in Southern Nevada, attendees will learn how online monitoring and awareness supports field activities and how to utilize social media in the ever-changing world of aquatics. This session will demonstrate ways to utilize digital surveillance responsibly and ethically to stay up to date with current trends.

title: Don't Underestimate the Intern: 20+ Real Projects That Added Value to EH Programs
info: Stretched budgets and limited staffing are common in environmental health, but student interns can help! This session shares 20+ real-world examples of intern-led projects that brought lasting value to EH programs—from data analysis and outreach to policy research. Learn what competencies students develop in accredited EH programs and how they translate into practical agency support. Hear insights from an experienced intern supervisor and an EH program director from an accredited university. Leave inspired, with project ideas and confidence that hosting an intern can be a win-win.

title: Establishing Standards of Practice for Legionella Outbreak Investigation
info: Outbreaks Aren’t the Time to Experiment—They’re the Time to Execute Legionella outbreaks demand swift, coordinated action—but most jurisdictions still lack formal guidance on what a “complete” response looks like. Join this call to action for national standards that put environmental health professionals in the lead and make mitigation and prevention consistent across the country.

title: Estimating the Impact of Water and Sanitation Services in Indian Country: a CDC-IHS collaboration
info: Environmental health programs face increased need to demonstrate impact. Estimation tools use existing data to predict health outcomes. They provide a middle ground between real world measurements and process measures, often more practical than real world measurements and more directly linked to health outcomes than process measures. In 2015, IHS approached CDC to request assistance with demonstrating their impact on health outcomes. An inter-agency agreement was signed, and that same year, an estimation tool was developed. The tool has since been validated by a 3rd party, formally adopted by DSFC leadership and is used annually to justify the IHS funding request to Congress. This session may be of interest to those who wish to develop an estimation tool as a way of reporting program impact and those who are interested in effective collaboration between federal government agencies.

title: Industry Relations: Meeting the Needs of Operators Through Evidence-Based Programs and Resource Planning
info: Food operators often feel overwhelmed by public health regulations, leading to confusion and tension during inspections. Northern Nevada Public Health (NNPH) Environmental Health Services transformed this friction into collaboration through a proactive, data-driven approach. By analyzing data from multiple sources of industry and consumer feedback, NNPH developed targeted, low-cost educational tools including a “Food Safety Inspection Bootcamp”, clarifying expectations and empowering operators with practical resources. The goal: improved compliance, stronger industry trust, and a more transparent and collaborative regulatory environment. In this presentation, attendees will learn replicable strategies to foster meaningful dialogue, reduce resistance, and design outreach programs that reflect the needs of the communities they serve—all while staying budget-conscious and impact-focused.

title: Negotiating Food Freedom Laws: How Illinois Public Health Got a Seat at the Table
info: Illinois Environmental Health helped shape food freedom laws by prioritizing food safety in collaboration with advocates. Their work on laws like the Cottage Food Law shows how public health can support safe, inclusive food policies.

title: Requiring Food Safety Certifications in Local Jurisdictions: Navigating Through the Hurdles and Politics
info: After nearly 10 years in the making, The Douglas County Health Department proposed a new ordinance included food safety certification requirements that successfully passed the Omaha City Council. This session will share the successes and failures, what worked versus what failed, and the do's and don'ts of creating law in a political subdivision.

title: Strengthening Environmental Health Preparedness through Community Meals Training in Disaster Response
info: In the context of disaster response, volunteer food providers play a critical role in community recovery, yet often lack formal training in food safety practices. This presentation details a collaborative initiative between the North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services and a partnering academic institution to develop and implement a targeted, plain-language food safety curriculum for non-traditional food handlers. Designed for delivery by public health professionals, the course integrates adult learning principles and outbreak-based case studies to enhance knowledge retention. Preliminary evaluation data from in-person trainings indicate significant gains in food safety literacy and self-reported confidence in applying safe practices. The session will highlight the development process, implementation outcomes, and implications for strengthening public health preparedness through community-based education.

title: What's in Your Well Water: Determining What Contaminants to Test For
info: When private well owners call with questions about water safety and contaminants, are you ready to answer? This session from the Private Well Class equips environmental health professionals with tools, data, and confidence to guide private well users through region-specific contamination concerns. Through real-world examples and interactive activities, you will learn how to access reliable resources, collaborate with peers, and empower private well owners. Please bring your phone or a laptop.

title: Coffee Break
info: Join us for coffee on the hotel lobby terrace

title: Acute Gastroenteritis Outbreak on a Cruise Ship Sailing to the United States
info: CDC’s Vessel Sanitation Program (VSP) works with the cruise industry to monitor and investigate outbreaks of acute gastroenteritis (AGE) on cruise ships. In December 2024, VSP received notification of a ship with a large AGE outbreak headed toward the United States. As this voyage and subsequent ones progressed, passenger and crew case counts rose to greater than 500. VSP performed an epidemiological and environmental health investigation and identified several environmental factors that contributed to the outbreak. VSP used the lessons learned during this outbreak to inform the cruise industry on additional norovirus prevention and mitigation recommendations during the remainder of the 2024-2025 norovirus season.

title: AI Agents: Beyond Chatbots
info: What if you could hand routine tasks to a tireless intern? AI Agents can do just that, summarize, reassign, draft, and more. Join us to explore the potential and the pitfalls in environmental health.

title: CDC’s Environmental Health Specialists Network (EHS-Net): Improving Restaurant Food Safety and Foodborne Outbreak Prevention
info: This session’s presenters represent Environmental Health Specialists Network (EHS-Net), a CDC-funded group of state and local health departments that conduct projects designed to improve restaurant food safety. Presenters will share innovative insights and tools on restaurant food safety and employee health and hygiene policies, mobile food vendors (e.g., food trucks), kitchen manager food certification, and food safety culture.

title: From Confusion to Compliance: Navigating the FDA Food Traceability Rule for Retailers and Foodservice Establishments
info: Retailers and foodservice establishments are facing a new era of accountability with the FDA’s Food Traceability Rule (FTR), issued under FSMA. While compliance may feel distant with the 2028 deadline, the complexities of recordkeeping, technology adoption, and operational changes demand early action. This session demystifies the FTR by exploring what it means for retail and restaurant operators—many of whom are navigating FSMA requirements for the first time. Drawing on research and expert insights from industry, academia, and public health, participants will gain practical strategies to prepare for compliance, identify common challenges, and build sustainable traceability systems that protect public health and strengthen consumer trust.

title: Future-Ready by Design: Strategic Workforce Planning for Dynamic Times
info: Struggling to meet growing demands with limited resources? You’re not alone and you’re not out of options. In this interactive 90-minute learning lab, discover how strategic workforce planning can turn constraints into opportunities. Designed for leaders in both industry and government, this session will help you rethink how roles are shaped, how talent is developed, and how transitions, whether planned or unexpected, can be part of a bigger, smarter workforce strategy. Forget cookie-cutter org charts. You’ll learn to design roles around strengths, align talent with future goals, and create development pathways that keep teams engaged and future-ready. With hands-on tools and practical case studies, you’ll leave empowered to build a resilient, agile workforce, even under pressure. Whether you’re facing hiring freezes, restructuring, or just want to get ahead of the curve, this lab is your blueprint for workforce planning that works. Don’t miss it!

title: Georgia’s Cutting-Edge Response to Childhood Lead Poisoning: A Statewide Model for Environmental Health Excellence
info: Georgia’s bold, technology-driven lead poisoning program sets a national benchmark, combining early intervention, statewide staffing, and real-time environmental data for unmatched prevention impact.

title: Investing in Impact: The Evolution of the RFFM Grant Program
info: This session will showcase the evolution and impact of the NEHA-FDA Retail Flexible Funding Model Grant Program (RFFM), a multi-million-dollar initiative advancing conformance with FDA’s Retail Program Standards. Since its inception, the program has invested nearly $30 million across 375 jurisdictions to support almost 2,000 projects, while reducing administrative burdens through an updated payment system. Now in its second cycle, RFFM is extending its reach through capacity building projects that have already engaged 100+ unfunded jurisdictions, strengthening networks and technical support nationwide. Join us to learn how data-driven strategies, strong partnerships, and continuous improvement are driving measurable progress in retail food safety and protecting communities from foodborne illness.

title: Mocktails, Potions, and Elixirs: Unapproved Food Additives in a Changing Landscape
info: Mocktails, potions and elixirs. What will they think of next? Manufacturers are cashing in on the sober curious movement. Dry January, Sober October, whatever you call it, these trends are popular, and industry has responded by offering up products to satisfy the consumers sober curiosity. The innovation doesn’t stop in the bar either. Mushroom coffees and herbal tonics are popping up in coffeeshops promising non-descript health benefits ranging from stress reduction to improved brain function. This presentation will focus on how unapproved food additives have found their way into our beverages. The challenge for regulators is to keep customers safe and informed in this changing beverage landscape. Attendees will gain a greater understanding of what can be added to food and beverages. With this new knowledge base regulators will be better equipped to educate operators and help them navigate these confusing waters.

title: Preparedness by Design: Putting GIS and Training to Work in the U.S. Virgin Islands
info: How can environmental public health (EPH) professionals make better use of data in emergencies? This session explores NEHA’s work with the U.S. Virgin Islands Department of Environmental Health to strengthen preparedness and decision-making through innovative data tools and training. Participants will learn about a new GIS fact sheet designed to highlight the capabilities of spatial data, hear lessons from tailored emergency response training, and gain insights from evaluation findings. The session highlights practical strategies that can be adapted in other jurisdictions to build a resilient, data-proficient EH workforce.

title: The Scale Changes but the Challenges Don't: Managing Environmental Health in Different Sized Jurisdictions
info: Whether serving a state, regional, or local community, environmental health professionals face many of the same obstacles—limited resources, complex governance, staffing constraints, and the need for effective contingency planning. What changes is the scale. This session brings together three presenters, each representing a different level of governmental public health, to share how these challenges are managed within their jurisdictions. Attendees will hear how priorities are set and policies implemented at the state level, how coordination and collaboration are balanced across regions, and how environmental health plays out in direct service to communities at the municipal level. By comparing approaches across these settings, the session will highlight common threads, innovative strategies, and lessons learned that can strengthen environmental health practice at any scale.

title: Barriers to Effective Lead Case Management in the Omaha Lead Superfund Site
info: This session explores the barriers families face in lead case management at the Omaha Lead Superfund site, including language challenges, fear over legal status, and systemic gaps in landlord accountability. Participants will learn culturally responsive strategies to improve engagement, reduce childhood lead exposure, and address health inequities in high-risk communities.

title: 2025 ITRC PFAS Team Update
info: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) are an emerging group of contaminants that present unique challenges in many areas, including widespread use and presence in the environment, sampling and analysis, fate and transport, and remedial approaches. This training session will provide an introduction to guidance documents and learning tools that the Interstate Technology and Regulatory Council (ITRC) has developed for regulators, site managers, facility owners, consultants, technology developers, stakeholders, and others.

title: A Guide for Public Health Inspectors to Understand Water Treatment Product Certifications
info: Do you understand which product certifications are required by Federal Law for drinking water treatment devices? Which product certifications are required by the Ten State Standards? Which product certification standards are less stringent, more stringent, or equivalent to these standards? This session will unravel all the confusion and overlapping product standards for drinking water treatment devices and provide a clear guide to inform inspectors on which certifications to look for.

title: Bringing Insecticide-Free Mosquito Control to Cities: Lessons Learned from Tropical Island Programs
info: This session uses tropical island mosquito-control programs as real-world case studies for insecticide-free vector management.
