Field data from the Maldives and the Philippines show how sustained adult mosquito removal can reduce mosquito populations by 50% to 90% and, under highly favorable conditions, support local elimination. The presentation then translates these lessons to urban “functional islands,” emphasizing practical deployment planning, surveillance-driven decision-making, realistic performance goals, and integration with larval source management and community engagement.

title: Building Environmental Health Capacity through Development of Environmental Health E-Learning Trainings on Climate Stressors and Environmental Hazards for Clinicians and Local Health Officials
info: Developing accessible and engaging training is essential for building capacity to address environmental health challenges in clinical and public health practice. The Massachusetts Bureau of Climate and Environmental Health created two e-learning series using Articulate Storyline and Articulate Rise, each tailored to distinct audiences. This session will present an adaptable workflow for developing digital trainings that bridge important concepts from core environmental health practice (e.g., exposure reduction) with healthcare and community perspectives. Lessons learned highlight the importance of early community engagement, tailoring content to local contexts, and leveraging e-learning platforms to create flexible, interactive modules that strengthen environmental health capacity.

title: Legionnaires' Disease -- Integrating Communicable Disease and Environmental Health Programs
info: In many state and local health Departments, Environmental Health staff are left out of any discussions regarding organizational response to Legionnaires' disease cases until an outbreak investigation is announced. This session will discuss the importance of a collaborative approach between communicable disease and environmental health teams at every stage of surveillance, data interpretation, cluster and outbreak thresholds, and defining the level of resources that will need to be delivered under different case scenarios. The sessions will discuss how NYC Health developed formal collaboration agreements between the environmental health and communicable disease teams to ensure coordinated and transparent expectations.

title: Mapping the Progress of Lead Poisoning Prevention Community Awareness Outreach in Douglas County (2021-2025): Achievements, Challenges, and Lessons Learned
info: Lead exposure remains a critical environmental health issue, especially for young children. Douglas County, Nebraska, has faced persistent challenges due to historic industrial activity, aging housing, and other sources of lead contamination. The Douglas County Health Department (DCHD) has made great efforts through its Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program (CLPPP), with a strong focus on outreach, education, and community engagement, which have increased the number of screenings and lowered the percentage of children with high blood lead levels. This session will map the reach of outreach activities from 2021 to 2025. Using a mix of spatial mapping, program data analysis, and staff insights, the presentation will highlight key challenges, successes, and lessons learned. Attendees will gain practical ideas for mapping program impact, overcoming outreach barriers, and applying these findings to strengthen similar environmental health efforts in their own jurisdictions.

title: New Recipe for Food Safety: Blending Management with Culture
info: The collaboration between Food Safety Management and Food Safety Culture, facilitated by strong leadership, is the key ingredient to ensuring that execution matters and that the industry can finally achieve a meaningful reduction in foodborne illness.

title: Promoting Building Occupant Safety and Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) During and Following Emergencies
info: We describe the EPA's Emergencies and Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) Program, which promotes healthier indoor air before, during, and following emergencies, such as wildfires, severe weather, floods, and power outages. This presentation will highlight program guidance and recommendations on safeguarding health and IAQ from emergencies, efforts to support EPA emergency response and recovery efforts, and available tools and resources to promote public awareness of emergencies and IAQ issues. Additionally, attendees will learn about EPA's Wildfire Smoke Preparedness in Community Buildings Grant Program, a federal grant program to support enhancing community wildfire smoke and IAQ resilience.

title: Lunch Break
info: Lunch Break

title: Advancing Public Health Preparedness in WUI Communities: Insights from the Wildfire Steering Committee
info: Join the National Network of Public Health Institutes as we dive into the findings and recommendations from the Wildland-Urban Interface (WUI) Wildfire Steering Committee. The steering committee guided the development and refinement of wildfire materials to ensure they are evidence-based, locally adaptable, and responsive to community and public agency needs. This session will feature a panel of committee members and will provide oversight and guidance for WUI communities, share curated recommendations and resources to support communities affected by wildfires.

title: Bridging Regulatory and Industry Practice: Environmental Public Health Risk-Based Food Safety Inspections and Active Managerial Control for Food Establishments
info: Transform your food establishment inspection approach with newly developed Risk-Based Inspection Guidance Resources that serve both regulators and industry. The NEHA Food Safety Program Committee will present ten comprehensive guidance documents designed to help environmental public health professionals prioritize violations while providing managers on duty with practical tools for achieving active managerial control. Based on FDA's Risk Factor Survey data, these dual-purpose resources address poor personal hygiene, equipment contamination, improper time/temperature control, and inadequate cooking practices. Learn how to implement systematic, evidence-based protocols that improve inspection consistency, strengthen industry compliance, and enhance regulatory effectiveness. This practical session provides immediately actionable tools for both regulatory and management applications.

title: Building Environmental Health Capacity to Address Hazards in Small Community Water Systems
info: Rhode Island Department of Health, New Mexico Department of Health, California Public Health Institute, Virginia Department of Health, and Wisconsin Department of Health and Human Services have varied projects to help protect public health by addressing environmental health hazards in small community water systems including updating source water assessments for SCWS, maintaining an on-call service for questions, identifying and testing homes with direct creek and lake intakes for contaminants, creating of a comprehensive GIS tool, and providing of mini-grants to local health departments. Panel presenters will discuss their projects in detail, challenges and barriers to completing the projects, success stories, and lessons learned from project implementation. They will discuss navigating regulatory challenges, working with partner agencies to address drinking water hazards, informing consumers, and resources that they have created.

title: Mastering Water Quality: Tackling Microbial Challenges in Facility Plumbing Systems
info: Ensuring high-quality water with sufficient chlorine residual is just the beginning. This session explores organic and biological factors—beyond pH, alkalinity, and phosphate—that degrade water quality in systems with intermittent flows. Through credible science and real-world case studies, we’ll examine why microbiology often drives persistent issues like lead, copper, taste, odor, and pathogen risks, particularly in systems where the residual is lost by the first morning draw. Discover practical strategies to make plumbing systems resistant to bacterial growth and gain actionable solutions to confidently tackle water quality challenges in your facility.

title: NCEH/ATSDR’s Perspective on Micro- and Nanoplastics in the Environment and Human Health Concerns
info: Environmental health practitioners need adequate resources and technical support to meet the demands of their job responsibilities to address issues related to emerging environmental contaminants of concern like MNPs. In the absence of those resources, the work that the Micro- and Nanoplastics and Human Health Working Group (MNPH2WG) is conducting at the NCEH and ATSDR, can assist practitioners in addressing community inquiries about the potential impact that exposures to MNPs in the environment pose on human health.

title: Overview of a Pilot Project Utilizing GIS Mapping Technology to Locate Mobile Food Establishments for Timely Food Safety Inspections
info: In a discussion of the development and implementation of a GIS mapping tool to help locate food trucks for timely food safety inspections we will examine a pilot project in one north Alabama county. We will explore the building of a coalition of vested interests that facilitated the application of this technological tool to not only address our regulatory goals but also provide useful information beneficial to all parties with interest.

title: Protecting Public Health and Improving State Aquatic Venue Regulations with a Free Web-based Dynamic Water Quality Simulation Tool
info: It is finally easy to accurately compare swimming pool regulatory strategies using a free webtool that calculates pathogen concentrations and human exposure over time based on a dynamic bather load. Want to find out how new regulations could make swimming venues 100-times safer than existing regulations?

title: Stopping Norovirus at Retail - Hands-on Tools for Safer Food Environments
info: Take home practical tools you can use tomorrow! This interactive session highlights the AFDO Healthy People 2030 Norovirus at Retail Project and partnerships with CFP and the Environmental Contaminants Workgroup. Attendees will test FDA’s digital decision-support app, work through a CDC outbreak case study, and tackle AFDO’s surface contamination challenge—equipping you with real-world strategies to prevent and respond to norovirus in retail foodservice.

title: The Big Bad Biofilm
info: The Big Bad Biofilm. Biofilms are notorious for complicating food safety, yet many questions about them remain unanswered. While wet surface biofilms have been studied for years, the emerging world of dry surface biofilms is only beginning to be understood. Are they lurking in food facilities? How do they form, and what risks do they pose? This session will dive into the mysteries of both wet and dry biofilms, highlight the gaps in our knowledge, and explore practical considerations for monitoring and control.

title: Working Together to Achieve More in the FDA Retail Program Standards
info: Discover how Virginia is advancing its FDA Retail Program Standards through strong collaboration between the Virginia Department of Health Central Office and 35 local health districts. This presentation highlights Virginia's approach, with insights from both central and local leaders. Learn about the tools, networking meetings, workshops, and teamwork driving progress statewide. Whether you’re involved at the state or local level, see how coordinated efforts are making a real difference in meeting FDA standards and improving retail food safety. Join us to explore practical strategies for moving the needle together.

title: Workplace Safety Controls for Risk Based Body Art Facility Inspections
info: Body piercing educator John Johnson, from the Association of Professional Piercers (APP), returns to speak to body art facility inspectors about their risk based inspections. This session will benefit all regulators who inspect body art facilities.

title: Empowering Communities: Air Monitors for Informed Health Decisions & Equity
info: Each spring, smoke from controlled burns in the Flint Hills drifts into Nebraska, negatively impacting air quality and public health. With limited statewide regulatory monitoring, many residents remain unaware of the risks posed by associated small particulate (PM2.5) pollution. Discover how strategic partnerships and CDC funding is changing the game with low-cost monitors, real-time data access, and public outreach. Learn how this initiative empowers communities with the information they need to live and breathe healthier.

title: Safer Science in Schools: Managing Risk Through Lab Rehab
info: Georgia lacks specific regulations for managing chemicals and hazardous waste in secondary school science labs, with vague training requirements and minimal guidance for schools. This regulatory gap contributes to chemical stockpiling, driven by under-funding, high teacher turnover, and reluctance to discard potentially useful materials. Aging chemicals and accumulating hazardous waste increase risks, especially as labels degrade and disposal becomes more difficult. These factors lead to preventable lab accidents. To address the issue, the Chemical Hazards Program of Georgia’s Department of Public Health is piloting a multi-agency cooperative program, informally called “Lab Rehab”, that offers chemical inventory support, waste removal, and lab safety training. Modeled after successful initiatives in other states, it aims to become the nation’s most comprehensive school lab safety program.

title: Evaluating How Exposure to Lead, Manganese, and PFAS in the Caregiving Environment Affect Neurodevelopmental Outcomes - The Preschool Environmental Experiences and Development Study
info: Children are exposed to many common chemicals in their day-to-day life. The first few years of life is a stage with very fast growth and brain development. Toddlers’ growing brains are particularly sensitive to chemical exposures. We conducted a study recruiting families and their child care centers to collect environmental samples, participate in the sample collection process, and learn about ways they can improve their environmental health. We studied metals and PFAS in children’s drinking water to better understand how these exposures might impact their early development. Specifically we show correlations between drinking water lead, manganese, and PFAS in caregiving environments in early childhood, and changes in neurodevelopment for preschoolers. In this study we recruited, educated, and enrolled approximately 300 children attending licensed childcare centers into the study.

title: My Top 7 Food Safety Go To's for Guiding Cannabis Quality Assurance
info: As cannabis-infused food products enter mainstream markets, the absence of harmonized safety standards poses a serious challenge to public health and regulatory consistency. For internal QA teams, it’s often an alphabet soup of overlapping regulations—state, federal, county, local, and internal policies—all pulling in different directions. Whether you're a seasoned QA professional or someone building compliance from scratch, it can be overwhelming to know where to start and what actually holds up under audit. This session walks through seven food safety principles I rely on daily to keep cannabis QA on track. Practical tools for managing risk, labeling and documentation that pass regulatory scrutiny. I’ll share how I apply these principles in real cannabis environments, where & with people who traditionally are not food safety (regulatory) focused. Attendees will leave with a clear, usable framework for cannabis QA

title: Onsite Wastewater System Repair/Replacement Grant in KS
info: Onsite wastewater system repair/replacement grants help protect water quality and constituents in the State of Kansas.

title: Speed & Simplicity Meets Accuracy: How MALDI-TOF Mass Spectrometry is Transforming Environmental Microbe Identification
info: CSI: Microbe—How MALDI-TOF Mass Spectrometry is a Real-World CSI Tool for Public Health Microbiology

title: Strengthening Practices for Environmental Health Practitioners
info: As an environmental health practitioner, searching for innovative solutions to emerging environmental health concerns can be a daunting task. Attending conferences, reading scientific literature, and building teams to strategically plan solutions can take time away from providing daily public health services to your community. With your help, CDC is building a platform for discovering, understanding, and sharing solutions to common and emerging environmental health threats. It involves gathering information, assessing, and sharing these solutions, which we call environmental public health (EPH) practices, to prevent exposure to environmental hazards and improve the health of people in all communities. For this platform, a practice is viewed as the implementation of an activity, intervention, program, project, campaign, or forum. Attend this session and develop familiarity with the NCEH’s efforts for building a web-based public repository of EPH evidence-supported practices that environmental health practitioners can reference for adoption and implementation.

title: Swimming in Uncertainty - Beach Water Quality Predictive Modeling and Public Health Communication
info: Traditional beach monitoring relies on bacterial testing, which can take up to 18-24 hours to produce advisories or beach closures, potentially leaving beachgoers exposed to contaminated waters. This presentation showcases an innovative solution that bridges critical data gaps in recreational water quality monitoring at Tybee Island, Georgia beaches. Using 20 years of beach monitoring data combined with weather and tidal information, this project is a predictive model integrated into an interactive public-facing dashboard. Built with R and deployed online, the platform provides beachgoers with real-time conditions, historical water quality trends, and forecasted bacterial levels—much like weather apps influence daily decisions. This public health education tool empowers beachgoers to make informed decisions about their recreational activities while demonstrating how environmental data visualization can transform reactive monitoring into proactive community engagement. The presentation will cover model development, dashboard features, and implications for coastal health communication strategies.

title: The Secondary Colonizer Hypothesis: A New Paradigm for Controlling Listeria Persistence
info: Why does Listeria monocytogenes defy conventional sanitation? This presentation introduces the Secondary Colonizer Hypothesis, a novel ecological framework explaining the pathogen's remarkable persistence in food processing environments (FPEs). We show that L. monocytogenes is not a generalist opportunist, but a specialized colonizer. It strategically exploits nutrient-rich central voids created when Gram-negative biofilms disperse. This strategy is enabled by a sophisticated metabolic switch: L. monocytogenes utilizes cellular debris (Phosphatidylethanolamine) as a carbon/nitrogen source and employs an iron sequestration system to outcompete competitors and survive disinfectants. We identify Veillonella as a critical bridging species that facilitates this succession. Understanding L. monocytogenes as an ecological specialist demands a paradigm shift in food safety, moving from reactive pathogen detection to proactive ecological niche management and revised sanitation protocols.

title: Coffee Break
info: Join us for coffee on the hotel lobby terrace

title: Come Pierce With Me!
info: "Come Pierce with Me!" is an immersive exploration into the world of body piercing, tailored for enthusiasts and newcomers alike. This session delves into the art and science of body piercing, covering essential tools, techniques, and safety protocols integral to the craft. Attendees will gain insight into various piercing styles, aftercare practices, and common myths and misconceptions. Through live demonstrations and interactive Q&A, "Come Pierce with Me!" offers a hands-on experience that highlights body piercing as both an art form and a professional discipline. Attendees will practice piercing on high-quality silicone body parts, simulating the experience of working with real clients in a safe, controlled environment. At the end of the session, each attendee will take home their own silicone model, complete with their personal piercing creation, a keepsake of the skills and insights gained. This workshop is a perfect opportunity for anyone curious about piercing to try it firsthand

title: Environmental Health Focused Decision Making in Cannabis
info: Adult-use cannabis has been legal in Nevada since 2017. Regulations and laws surrounding the control of cannabis have changed many times since then, even more dynamic has been the industry itself. Decision making in an industry without federal oversight or without a “federal code” to model from can prove to be tricky. Where can cannabis regulators to look for guidance? This presentation will focus on how regulators in Nevada have used a variety of resources that draw on environmental health principles for regulatory and policy decisions. Examples will be presented on how our team has looked all directions (“in”, “out”, “up”, and “sideways”) to find a tapestry of solutions for making public health decisions in a new and dynamic industry. Additionally, three practical decision-making tools will be presented with explanation on how they can be used in program development: risk assessment, stakeholder analysis and decision matrices.

title: Environmental Health in Government: What's happened? What's next?
info: In this session, members will learn about what has happened in Congress, the Administration and the States affecting environmental health. Congress has faced a tumultuous year, with the Republican majority working to implement the Administration's vision for environmental health, and the minority Democrats attempting to influence that vision. States, in contrast, have enacted policies that embrace the Administration's MAHA movement, making the states at the forefront of these efforts. Come hear from the government affairs team at NSF and NEHA discuss what has happened on environmental health since the elections in 2024, how has the Administration's vision for environmental health been implemented, and what will the MAHA movement mean for the states and federal agencies.

title: From Thousands of Guests to One Standard: Food Safety Stewardship in Large-Scale Events on the Las Vegas Strip
info: Las Vegas resorts serve as some of the most complex food and beverage operations in the world, where thousands of meals are prepared and delivered in a single sitting. Maintaining food safety in these high-volume environments requires more than compliance—it demands innovation, precision, and seamless coordination. This session draws on anonymized case examples from major Strip operations to explore how stewarding teams uphold safety and efficiency under extreme time pressure. Key topics include developing multilingual training programs that bridge language barriers, coordinating responsibilities across stewarding, culinary, and engineering teams, and implementing proactive measures to reduce cross-contamination risks. Attendees will also examine how high turnover, on-call labor, and union-defined job scopes impact food safety culture. Participants will leave with practical models for scalable training, communication strategies for diverse teams, and coordination tools that strengthen both operational performance and public health outcomes in large-scale hospitality environments.

title: Healthy Schools: Connecting Data, Policy, and Action for Safer Learning Environments
info: The 2025 State of Environmental Health in Pennsylvania Schools Report, led by Women for a Healthy Environment (WHE), weaves together statewide data, community insights, and policy perspectives to reveal a complex tapestry of environmental hazards and inequities in schools. Between 2018 and 2022, WHE analyzed 166 school districts across six regions, examining 21 environmental indicators—from lead and radon to mold, air quality, and PFAS. Findings show that while some progress has been made, overall compliance with health and safety best practices has declined, particularly in older and under-resourced schools. The report underscores how aging infrastructure and limited funding threaten children’s health and learning. Through data-driven recommendations and advocacy tools, WHE calls for stronger policies and collective action to ensure every child in Pennsylvania learns and thrives in a healthy, safe environment.

title: Local Leadership, Statewide Change: Food Safety Collaboration Across Counties
info: This presentation highlights the collaborative efforts between Mecklenburg County Public Health and Cabarrus Health Alliance, demonstrating how partnerships between counties of different sizes can drive meaningful improvements in food safety across North Carolina. Rooted in a history of strong local leadership and a commitment to public health, this partnership reflects how operational differences and shared goals can lead to innovative solutions that benefit communities statewide. The NC Retail Program Standards Network plays a vital role in supporting this collaboration, offering a framework for continuous improvement, knowledge exchange, and consistency. Through joint initiatives, resource sharing, and strategic coordination, Mecklenburg and Cabarrus have achieved outcomes that extend beyond their jurisdictions. This session will explore lessons learned, the importance of leadership, and the ongoing evolution of food safety as a dynamic and community-driven public health priority.

title: Sampling a Healthcare Facility Water System for Legionella: A Standardized Protocol Presentation
info: A Legionnaires’ outbreak in a healthcare facility demands a decisive public health response. For eight years, the NYSDOH team has refined a rigorous, standardized, 4-step investigative protocol to ensure stronger, more consistent public health outcomes. This protocol provides essential tools, including detailed checklists and critical insight into Water Management Plan (WMP) compliance for high-risk systems. This presentation walks participants through the field-tested process: situation assessment, sampling, data readings, and WMP evaluation. Learn strategic methods for identifying exposure sources and integrating environmental data with clinical results (e.g., qPCR) for definitive confirmation. Attend this session to gain the essential sampling threads needed to strengthen your community's Environmental Health Tapestry and conduct efficient, targeted Legionnaires’ disease investigations.

title: Stopping Poolside Perils: Inspectors’ Guide to Chemical Hazard Prevention
info: A joyful pool day can turn toxic. Choking fumes and burning eyes from chemical mishaps threaten lives. As environmental health inspectors, you can curb these preventable hospitalizations. Join our NEHA session to master chemical safety in pools and spas. Learn secure storage, proper dosing, and ventilation checks to prevent deadly gas releases. Case studies reveal why training and maintenance failures spark disasters. With practical checklists, spot risks like improper labeling or faulty feeders. Empower yourself to save lives, prevent hospitalizations, and ensure every swim is safe. Your vigilance makes the difference. Join us to make waves in safety!

title: Weaving Resilience: Bridging Environmental Health and Emergency Management to Strengthen All-Hazards Response
info: As disasters grow in frequency and complexity, state and local systems must sustain critical functions under reduced federal support. This session, aligned with NEHA’s 2026 theme “Woven Strong: The Environmental Health Tapestry,” explores how Environmental Health Officers (EHOs) trained in Emergency Management (EM) can strengthen preparedness, response, and recovery through a unified operational model. By identifying key crosswalks between EH and EM in planning, operations, and decision-making, this session introduces “Public Health Resilience Operations Network (PHRON)” a framework that trains, embeds, and empowers EH professionals within emergency management structures. Participants will learn how to integrate EH expertise into EOCs, apply EH data to EM decision-making, and build multidisciplinary teams capable of leading under constrained conditions. The result: more resilient, self-reliant communities ready to meet the demands of future all-hazards events.

title: When Outreach Doesn't Work and How to Move Forward: Lessons from Private Well Programs
info: Environmental health practitioners working with private wells often need to develop an outreach program to educate and motivate private well users to test their wells. This session shares lessons learned through our sustained collaboration with state and local health departments to help other safe water programs improve their outreach. With improved outreach, state and local health departments can better protect private well users from environmental health hazards.

title: Why Data & IT Literacy Will Rival Inspection Skills This Decade
info: Lead the future of EH: data, AI, and workforce skills beyond inspections. EH leadership is shifting and inspection skills must now be matched with data literacy and AI readiness. Learn how to equip your workforce with a competency matrix, governance tools, and strategies from real-world modernization projects.

title: A Case Study of Chloramine Exposure During a Youth Swim Meet
info: A regional youth swim meet became an unexpected case study in indoor air quality and pool safety. Learn how local public health officials responded to a chloramine exposure affecting hundreds, what they uncovered through investigation, and how it reinforced the need for adopting the Model Aquatic health Code and resulting policy changes for better prevention and preparedness.

title: A Comparison of Passive and Active Tick Surveillance Data to Assess Sampling Differences in the Tick Inventory via Citizen Science
info: The Tick Inventory via Citizen Science (TICS), launched by the Marshfield Clinic Research Institute in 2024, engages the public in tick submission, complementing standard active surveillance. Wisconsin TICS submissions from 2024 (n≈5,661) were compared with state-led tick drags (n≈1,462) using negative binomial regression. Ixodes scapularis, a key Lyme disease vector, was underrepresented in TICS submissions (IRR=0.26, p<0.001), and nymphal ticks were slightly fewer (IRR=0.73, p=0.055). Submissions increased with distance from the institute (IRR=1.003, p=0.006). These findings demonstrate that citizen science provides human exposure data that complements active surveillance. Integrating both approaches enhances tick-borne disease risk assessment and promotes broader public engagement in surveillance efforts.

title: A New Tool for Assessing PFAS Risk in Private Well Water
info: Private well users face unique challenges in assessing and managing exposure risks from PFAS in drinking water. We used publicly available data sets to map potential PFAS sources and private well use across the U.S. We developed a conceptual risk framework that considers multiple potential sources at the same time, incorporating factors such as size, reliability, density, and proximity of sources to private wells. We included risks associated with the land application of biosolids in rural areas. The risk scores generated by the framework were validated against PFAS concentrations from 270 private wells in four U.S. states, confirming the framework’s reliability and accuracy in estimating contamination risks. We then developed an interactive web mapper to visualize risk of PFAS contamination in private well water, including a hazard index, private well densities, and source point locations. This session will review the risk framework and demonstrate the interactive web mapper.

title: Associations between HeatRisk Levels and Cardiovascular Mortality Outcomes, Contiguous United States, 2005-2023
info: Exploring Associations Between HeatRisk Levels and Cardiovascular Mortality Outcomes

title: Environmental and Health Surveillance Following the East Palestine, OH Train Derailment: A Community-Engaged Approach
info: "The February 2023 train derailment in East Palestine, Ohio released vinyl chloride and other hazardous chemicals, creating urgent need for real-time environmental and health surveillance. This NIEHS-funded, time-sensitive study integrates environmental sampling, liver health monitoring, pediatric health concerns, and community perspectives to assess the impact of chemical exposures. The research is grounded in a community-engaged approach, guided by a Community Advisory Board and supported by trained Community-Based Researchers who assisted with recruitment and sampling. This multidisciplinary effort demonstrates the importance of community engagement in disaster response and underscores the need for advanced biomarker analysis and continued surveillance to address environmental health crises."

title: Fiscal Year 2025: State of the Vessel Sanitation Program
info: For more than 40 years, the CDC’s Vessel Sanitation Program (VSP) has worked with cruise industry partners to protect the health and safety of crew, passengers, and shoreside communities from the introduction, transmission, and spread of acute gastroenteritis (AGE). Fiscal Year (FY) 2025 was full of historical milestones for VSP, with the greatest number of operational inspections conducted since FY 2015, four construction inspections conducted, the greatest number of onboard AGE elevations responded to, and the publication of the 2025 Environmental Public Health Standards and the 2025 Construction Standards.

title: Measuring and Improving Inspection Program Efficiency in New York City: Strategies and Challenges
info: Since the COVID-19 pandemic, the New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene’s food inspection program has faced challenges meeting annual inspection goals, largely due to understaffing. Recognizing that staffing shortages would continue straining operations, our program needed to answer a critical question: how can we improve program efficiency? To answer this question and provide actionable solutions, we 1) developed metrics to measure program efficiency 2) assessed how efficiency has changed over time 3) and identified key variables affecting efficiency. We completed this analysis and created reporting tools that clarified trends in program efficiency. While some stakeholders embraced the new tools and insights, others were hesitant to integrate them into daily workflows. Our presentation highlights strategies used to measure program efficiency, showcase reporting tools developed to improve efficiency and enhance decision-making, share lessons learned in promoting adoption, and discuss next steps for embedding these tools into food safety operations.

title: Needle(s) in a Haystack: Norovirus Illnesses Associated with Raw Oyster Consumption
info: Traceback investigations can be like finding a needle in a haystack, but what happens when there are two needles? In late 2024, Maricopa County’s Environmental-Related Illness Program investigated over 30 norovirus illnesses linked to raw oysters from Washington State and British Columbia, all served at the same restaurant chain with three locations within Maricopa County. This session will highlight the investigative process, from complaint detection and interviews to analyzing shellstock tags, invoices, and raw bar cards. Attendees will gain practical strategies for navigating multi-source, high-volume outbreaks, and see firsthand how careful investigation and strong recordkeeping can uncover complex outbreak patterns.

title: Pediatric Preparedness for High-Consequence Infectious Diseases: Lessons from COVID-19 and Ghana
info: Children are often overlooked in outbreak preparedness. Drawing on lessons from COVID-19 and recent outbreaks in Ghana, this presentation highlights strategies to close pediatric preparedness gaps and strengthen community resilience. What can we learn from COVID-19 and Ghana’s Ebola/Marburg experience to better prepare children for future public health emergencies in underserved areas in the US? This session explores pediatric-centered strategies that promote equity and resilience. Preparedness plans often overlook the unique needs of children during outbreaks. This presentation explores practical, evidence-based approaches to pediatric preparedness that can enhance public health resilience.

title: Safe Materials for Body Piercing Jewelry: A Public Health Perspective for Environmental Health Inspectors
info: Equip inspectors with tools to assess body piercing jewelry safety using ASTM/ISO standards, the APP minimum standards and best practices for public health.

title: Using Community Navigators to Improve Well Water Safety
info: Millions of U.S. households rely on private wells, yet few test or treat their water for contaminants. The Be Well Intervention uses trained community navigators to help families test their wells, interpret results, and adopt effective treatment strategies. Learn how this innovative, community-centered approach improved well water safety in rural Oregon and offers a scalable model for protecting public health.

title: Honoring Excellence in Environmental Health
info: Begin the evening by recognizing the individuals making a lasting impact across our field. Join colleagues in celebrating this year’s award recipients, be inspired by their contributions, and be part of the moment that highlights what excellence in environmental health looks like. Then continue the conversation and connections at the networking event that follows.

title: EH Networking Social
info: Mix and mingle with your fellow environmental health professionals! Enjoy an evening of engaging conversations, lively entertainment, tasty bites, and all around fun. Take advantage of this spirited gathering to make connections in an exciting atmosphere. Dinner is included with your full conference registration This event will be held offsite at KC Live!

title: Career Connections Lounge
info: Your space to network, explore careers, and make meaningful connections. What you'll find: ✔ A job posting bulletin board – find or share open roles ✔ A quiet meeting space for career conversations ✔ Opportunities for informal interviews and networking How It Works: Post a job? Pin it to the board. Seeking a job? Check the board & connect through the app. Need a quiet spot to talk? Come on in. Use the conference app to schedule chats with hiring representatives. Open all conference long!

title: Environmental Health Photo Exhibition
info: The Environmental Public Health Photography Contest returns for its fourth year at the 2026 AEC, showcasing the power of imagery to tell the story of environmental health. Under this year’s theme, EH in View – Seeing What Matters, the exhibit highlights compelling visuals from our members that capture the challenges, impacts, and everyday work of the environmental health profession. From fieldwork moments to community impacts, each photograph invites viewers to see the issues that shape environmental health. The exhibit will be on display throughout the AEC for all attendees to experience and enjoy. Join us Tuesday evening at 5:00 pm, for the announcement of our contest winners!

title: Member Town Hall Breakfast
info: Join NEHA leadership for our annual Town Hall. Hear updates on NEHA’s recent initiatives and priorities and take part in the conversation by asking questions and sharing your feedback. Breakfast will be served at 6:45 am with the Town Hall Session starting promptly at 7:00 am.

title: Registration
info: Stop by the Registration Desk to check in to the conference and pick up your badge. Then, visit the Info Table to ask questions about the conference, get directions, collect badge ribbons, and obtain information on the various programs and services offered by NEHA.

title: Breathing Easier: Leveraging Rideshare and Air Quality Education to Advance Community Health in Shelby County, TN
info: Air pollution and traffic-related emissions remain key public health challenges in Shelby County, TN. This session will highlight how the Shelby County Health Department’s Air Quality Improvement Branch integrates transportation demand management and community education to improve air quality and promote healthier commuting behaviors. Through initiatives such as the Memphis Area Rideshare Program (MARS), Air Quality Flag Program, Stop at the Click campaign, and a newly developed Employer Rideshare Toolkit, the department has created a replicable model for reducing emissions, raising awareness, and empowering residents, schools, and employers to take action on high-pollution days. Participants will explore real-world strategies, learn from interactive case scenarios, and walk away with practical tools for linking transportation and air quality education within their own communities. This session offers an actionable roadmap for embedding sustainability, health equity, and behavior change into local public health practice.

title: Designing Accessible, Safe and Affordable Homes Through Universal Design for Families with Special Needs or Disabilities
info: Research has demonstrated how the home environment can play an important part in the health and safety of residents. Home-based health and safety hazards are especially important for residents who have disabilities or special needs affecting their accessibility in the home environment. Habitat for Humanity of Greater Memphis (HFHGM) is working with the Green & Healthy Homes Initiative (GHHI) to implement and evaluate an innovative program model, in which new homes will be constructed using universal design principles to ensure that residents with accessibility needs can live and thrive in their homes. This presentation will provide an overview of the program model, and report on pre/post data to highlight how the pilot changed to households’ home environments, and how those changes affect resident health, safety, and wellbeing. Results from the evaluation will provide early data for the business case for healthcare and other stakeholders to support this housing model.

title: From Data to Story: Tribal Approaches to Community Environmental Health Assessments in the Northwest
info: Discover how Northwest Tribes are using interactive Story Maps to connect environmental data, GIS technology, and Traditional Ecological Knowledge. This session will showcase community and state-level profiles that highlight Tribal strengths, challenges, and responses to climate change—offering practical insights into how mapping tools can guide program development, climate planning, and community engagement.

title: Know the Score: Challenges and Benefits of a Health Inspection Scoring System
info: Discover how a local health department strengthened food safety culture in their community through real-world application of the NACCHO/Newton Grading System Toolkit. This session highlights stakeholder engagement strategies, data standardization, communications innovations, key outcomes, lessons learned, and future plans to build a more resilient, data-driven food safety system at the local level.

title: Legal and Policy Strategies for States to Promote Environmental Justice
info: In 2025, the new administration disavowed environmental justice, closing environmental justice and health equity offices in federal agencies and blocking funds for environmental justice initiatives. In contrast, a number of states adopted, maintained, or strengthened laws or policies to advance environmental justice. A coalition of state attorneys general engaged in a robust defense of the legality of governmental programs to advance environmental justice. This session will highlight a policy surveillance analysis of state laws intended to advance environmental justice and to reduce health disparities associated with differences in access to clean air, clean water, and other healthy soil. The presentation will summarize results and highlight trends and preliminary observations.

title: Multi Agency Approach to Combat a Surge of Unpermitted Pop-Up Street Food Vendors
info: In 2024, Pima County experienced an unprecedented rise in unpermitted street food vendors, all of whom were selling the popular dish al pastor tacos. Despite multiple violation notices and cease-and-desist orders, these vendors continued operating in public without the required permits and in an unsanitary manner. As a result, the County partnered with local enforcement agencies to address the sale of unsafe food by these vendors.

title: PFAS Sampling in the National Park Service
info: The National Park Service (NPS) created a team to evaluate PFAS levels in 1,845 drinking water systems at 430 NPS units throughout the country. The NPS team established a novel approach to assess all the drinking water systems, which included improving data accuracy prior to testing, developing a consistent sampling procedure for operators, overseeing quality assurance of sampling results for accuracy, resampling, and designing and managing an NPS-wide tracking tool to provide real-time information on PFAS results.

title: Rebuilding Trust Before the Crisis: Advancing Risk Communication Frameworks for Environmental Emergencies
info: Environmental emergencies demand more than a rapid response they require trust built long before the crisis. This session explores proactive communication frameworks that help environmental health professionals strengthen credibility, reduce misinformation, and prepare communities for emergencies. Drawing on real-world case studies and the CDC’s “Be First, Be Right, Be Credible” framework, participants will learn how to use technology, cultural competence, and collaboration to enhance readiness and rebuild public confidence before disaster strikes.

title: The HACCP Cookbook: Recipes for Safety from the Kitchen to the Global Workplace
info: Food safety extends far beyond the kitchen, it’s shaped by HVAC, plumbing, logistics, packaging, sanitation, and even IT. In The HACCP Cookbook, Lisa A. Burke CP-FS, OHST, reframes Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) as a framework for every system that touches our food. Through HACCP’s five steps and seven principles, Burke shows how monitoring, verification, corrective actions, and documentation prevent risks across the food chain. A broken refrigerator truck, a power outage, or poor airflow can compromise safety as much as undercooked food. By linking hazards to actions and records, HACCP becomes more resilient, comprehensive, and collaborative. Ultimately, food safety works best when operators, engineers, suppliers, and auditors share responsibility. This book offers the recipe for protecting not only what we eat, but also consumer well-being worldwide.

title: Using an RFFM Capacity Building Grant to Coordinate the WV Retail Program Standards Collaborative
info: Learn how a local health department (LHD) successfully applied for an RFFM Capacity Building Grant that will bring them $300,000 in funding over three years. Learn how they then helped many LHDs in WV to apply for and receive RFFM grants of their own. Learn how they developed a statewide WV Retail Program Standards (RPS) Collaborative to provide help and support to those enrolled in the RPS. The WV RPS Collaborative meets monthly for training on the RPS and mutual accountability. Learn how they also used the Capacity Building Grant to collaborate with a NC LHD to hold a Self-Assessment Workshop. Learn how they developed model policies that LHDs could adopt in order to meet the policy requirements of Standards 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. The WV RPS Collaborative also developed monthly food safety newsletters for both industry and consumers and conducted audits of completed standards.

title: What is the Food Safety Management System CFP Committee Up To?
info: Discover how the Food Safety Management System Committee’s latest findings, recommendations, the start of a national AMC Toolbox, and guidance are set to influence the future of how industry and regulators can comply and assess food safety standards!

title: Safe Waters, Strong Communities: Advancing Aquatic Health Regulation in the Pacific Islands
info: The Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands (CNMI) launched its first aquatic health regulatory program to protect swimmers and strengthen tourism safety. Through stakeholder engagement, adaptation of the Model Aquatic Health Code, and hands-on training for inspectors and operators, CNMI developed its own aquatic health code and inspection framework. This session will showcase the three-phase process—planning, code development, and training—while highlighting lessons learned in building local capacity, engaging industry, and addressing unique island challenges. Participants will gain insights into how partnerships and technical assistance can drive sustainable public health improvements.

title: 2026 Food Code Unpacked: What’s New, What’s Significant - Policy in Practice
info: Major updates are coming in the 2026 Food Code- are you ready? This session offers a comprehensive overview of the most significant changes and offers actionable steps for implementation.

title: Advancing Local Environmental Health Through Satellite Data: A Collaborative Partnership Between Harris County Public Health and NASA’s DEVELOP Program
info: Discover how Harris County Public Health partnered with NASA’s DEVELOP Program to integrate satellite-based Earth observation data into local air quality monitoring and health decision-making. This session will highlight how combining remote sensing tools with community health priorities can strengthen environmental surveillance, advance data-driven policy, and promote health equity at the local level.

title: Breathing Better at Home: Empowering Public Action on Indoor Air Quality
info: Join us for a dynamic panel exploring how community-engaged research and low-cost interventions are transforming indoor air quality in residential spaces. Three innovative studies—focused on radon, PM₂.₅ in homes with asthma patients, and HVAC filter forensics—reveal how education, monitoring, and public health training empower residents to take control of their home environments. Panelists will share actionable insights on pollution monitoring and measurement, the impact of indoor interventions paired with education, and the amplifying impact of collaboration with local organizations. This panel is ideal for government partners, academics, and nonprofits seeking to support community-engaged strategies for improving indoor air quality.

title: Building Bridges: Advancing Public Health Equity and Growing Connections Through the Hispanic Outreach Initiative
info: In 2024, the Pima County Health Department’s Consumer Health and Food Safety (CHFS) Division launched the Hispanic Outreach Initiative, a cross-divisional strategy to improve communication, strengthen trust, and advance equity for the county’s largest ethnic group. While all communities of color face disproportionate health inequities, CHFS prioritized outreach to Hispanic residents, who represent a significant portion of the population. This initiative is rooted in language access, cultural competency, and staff-led collaboration, and aligns with both FDA Retail Program Standard 7 and a Pima County resolution declaring racial and ethnic health inequities a public health crisis. Through Spanish speaking staffing, protected planning time, community partnerships, and the Spanish translation of key resources, CHFS is actively shifting from enforcement-driven approaches to an education-first, equity-centered model. The result is a more inclusive, resilient public health system, one that prioritizes community voice, builds trust, and improves outcomes for underserved populations.

title: Designing for Success: MCESD’s Hybrid Training Model Aligned with FDA Standard 2
info: Curious about standard 2 requirements and how to implement those requirements into a new hire training format? How about integrating those requirements into a fast-paced hybrid new hire training format? Through evaluation and adaptation, this can be done while streamlining the process to meet Program Standard 2, which enhances the ability for Environmental Health Specialists to be well prepared to conduct risk-based inspections in a standardized format. In this session, you will learn about practical approaches, curriculum integration and utilizing program resources and succession programs geared towards meeting Retail Program Standard 2 requirements.

title: Evaluating the Public Health Impact of FDA Retail Program Standards: A 10-Year Analysis of Outbreaks and Cases
info: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s Retail Food Regulatory Program Standards (RPS) are designed to enhance food safety through consistent inspections, workforce training, and data-driven oversight. Using ten years of national data from the CDC’s National Outbreak Reporting System (2014-2023), this study examined whether jurisdictions participating in RPS experienced lower foodborne illness burden. Counties were grouped as Active (meeting one or more standards), Not Active (enrolled but meeting none), or Not Enrolled. Active counties consistently showed fewer outbreaks and illness cases than other groups, and statistical models confirmed a clear, stepwise relationship between higher RPS engagement and reduced outbreak incidence. These results were stable across multiple analyses and years. Findings highlight the value of ongoing investment in standardized food safety programs, particularly in socially vulnerable communities, and underscore the importance of continued collaboration among local, state, and federal partners to advance risk-based retail food protection.

title: Keeping Turnover Without Compromise: COSA Retrofit Audits Using VS Flow Control & TDH Methods to Safeguard Water Quality
info: Can variable-speed pump programming meet mandated turnover without compromising water quality? San Antonio’s multi-pool retrofit shows how. This session translates a field-tested workflow—TDH measurements, pump-curve/flow-meter cross-checks, and VS flow-hold validation—into an inspection-ready protocol any health department can use. Results: turnover maintained, clarity protected, and documented savings (≈707,517 kWh/year; ≈$62,847/year) with an ≈0.7-year post-rebate payback. Equally important, the audits exposed hidden problems that undermine public health—aging or incorrect filter media, weak backwash capability, and inaccurate meters—and show how to fix them. Attendees will leave with a one-page checklist, programming guardrails, and simple QA steps that make compliance defensible during plan review and routine inspections.

title: Leading Beyond Compliance: Building Food Safety IN Through Leadership, Culture, and Best In Class Continuous Improvement
info: Food safety cannot be inspected into existence. It must be intentionally built into systems, culture, and leadership. This engaging session explores how professionals can move beyond compliance to design sustainable food safety programs driven by accountability, communication, and continuous improvement. Drawing on decades of experience across government, corporate, and third-party programs, Bill Flynn and Audris Barnes reveal how leadership behavior and system design can replace reactive inspection cycles with proactive culture building. Through real-world examples of health departments and restaurant groups recognized for prevention and transformation, attendees will see how data, behavior, and influence can make food safety measurable and meaningful. Participants will also learn how to effectively communicate the value of food safety to gain genuine C-suite buy-in. Leave equipped with practical tools to build food safety in, not inspect it in, and to lead beyond compliance through collaboration and continuous improvement.

title: Private Well Water Safety in the Wake of Disaster: Tennessee’s Emergency Response Using At-Home Test Kits
info: In September 2024, historic flooding from a hurricane devastated East Tennessee, threatening the safety of private well water for thousands. In response, the Tennessee Department of Health, in partnership with other state agencies, launched an emergency initiative using at-home coliform bacteria test kits—originally piloted through the CDC-funded Environmental Health Capacity Program. This rapid response effort provided critical water safety resources to impacted residents, especially in rural and underserved communities. By leveraging Geographic Information System data, multilingual outreach, and pre-established community relationships, the program reached thousands of potentially affected privately owned wells. This innovative approach highlights how Tennessee is building capacity to respond quickly to disasters while addressing long-standing gaps in private water system regulation and safety.

title: Secondary Treatment Systems in Aquatic Venues
info: UV, Ozone, AOP - Oh My! Hear about Secondary Treatment systems such as UV, Ozone, and Advanced Oxidation Potential (AOP) and the when's and how's of their use in aquatic venues.

title: Splash Pad Outbreaks in the United States: A 25-Year Review
info: This session characterizes splash pad-associated outbreaks reported to CDC that occurred between 1997-2022. Cryptosporidium, an extremely chlorine tolerant pathogen, was the leading cause of outbreaks 40 (67%), cases 9,622 (91%), and hospitalizations 123 (81%). Public health officials and the aquatics sector can use the findings in this report to promote the prevention of splash pad-associated outbreaks (e.g., recommended user behaviors) and guide the construction, operation, and management of splash pads. Public health practitioners and the aquatics sector also can collaborate to voluntarily adopt CDC’s Model Aquatic Health Code recommendations to prevent waterborne illness associated with splash pads.

title: A Meta-Analysis of Chlorine Sensitive RWIs in Swimming Pools and Hot Tubs
info: According to a 2023 CDC disease burden estimate, more than ninety percent of illnesses in recreational water are caused by chlorine-sensitive pathogens. This indicates that facility management failures—such as insufficient chlorination, poor filtration, and inadequate staff training—are responsible for most recreational water illnesses (RWIs). Numerous case studies are available, but a systematic analysis of these outbreaks has not been reported. This meta-analysis will help categorize operational failures and provide public health officials with targeted inspection items to help reduce RWI outbreaks.

title: Coffee Break
info: Join us for coffee on the hotel lobby terrace

title: From Enrollment to Excellence: Leveraging Mentorship to Navigate Today’s Retail Food Regulatory Challenges
info: Discover how retail food regulatory programs are navigating today’s challenges while advancing conformance with the Retail Program Standards. Hear directly from NEHA-FDA RFFM Mentorship Program participants, join interactive table-topic discussions, ask questions, and take home practical tools to help your program enroll, progress, and sustain success in the Standards.

title: Hydroponics and Food Safety
info: Hydroponics, the technique of growing plants using a water-based nutrient solution rather than soil, is becoming a more conventional way of growing fresh produce. Often used in small-scale operations in retail food establishments, hydroponic large-scale operations are prevailing in urban areas to be able to grow a large variety of produce in a small amount of space. Large-scale operations that are equivalent to multiple acres of farmland have not widely been seen in the Midwest, so when an individual located in St Louis wanted to discuss opening a large-scale hydroponic operation and retail food establishment, Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services was quickly notified. A collaboration effort amongst State agencies and industry ensured that safe food product was being grown and sold to consumers. This presentation will be given in joint with Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services, Missouri Department of Agriculture, and industry representative from Neon Greens.

title: Intersectionality Between Foodservice Operations, Agriculture, and Public Health
info: Hyperlocal restauranteurs grow their own produce and/raise livestock and hence source food from their own “backyard”. Restaurants and farms are overseen by different regulatory agencies. Health inspections are conducted by local city or county public health agencies; and in some states, produce safety inspections are conducted by the State Department of Agriculture. The public health officials have backgrounds that relate to their specialty and cannot be cross trained since the scope of their individual job task is significant. However, in the context of hyperlocal restaurant operations, these tasks intersect since the restaurant operator is also the farm operator. The overarching goal of this study was to explore the intersection of public health, agriculture, and foodservice operations within the context of hyperlocal restaurants. This session will highlight this delta through interviews conducted directly with stakeholders (restauranteurs and public health officials) and provide important practitioner takeaways.

title: Navigating the Retail Frontier of Unregulated Psychoactive Products
info: Mainstream retail's rapidly evolving landscape of unregulated substances—including mushroom chocolate 'blends,' THC hemp gummies, and kratom concentrates—now poses a critical challenge for public health and regulatory authorities.

title: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) in Freshwater Fish and Potential Health Risks from Fish Consumption
info: Former military facilities located adjacent to waterbodies may have contaminated nearby surface water and fish with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) from long-term use and discharge of aqueous film-forming foam. Fish samples were collected from locations at different distances both downstream and upstream from bases adjacent to Lake Worth and Lower Leon Creek in Texas. The results showed total PFAS levels in fish from downstream sites have significantly higher tissue levels of PFAS compared to fish from upstream locations. Long-term and regular fish consumption of freshwater drum and largemouth bass containing PFAS may pose noncancer risk for subsistence fishers at Lake Worth. While consumption of PFAS in common carp, largemouth bass, redbreast, gizzard shar and grey redhorse has the potential to cause noncancer health risk for adults, children less than 6 years, and subsistence fishers at Lower Leon Creek.

title: Residential Investigations of Legionnaires’ Disease in Southern Nevada
info: What happens when the source of Legionella transmission is your home or place of work? This session’s presenter represents the SNHD’s Environmental Health Division that conducts residential investigations of Legionnaires’ Disease. The presenter will share methods of determining occupational exposure sites, potential exposures through usage of medical devices, and recreational exposures through hot tubs. Recommendations for water systems contaminated with Legionella bacteria and steps moving forward will also be discussed.

title: Results of the 2019-2022 FDA National Retail Risk Factor Study
info: Got compliance issues? This track will present trends in observed Food Code risk factor violations in retail food store delis from 2019-2022.

title: Risk-Based WNV Surveillance in the Sonoran Desert: GIS, Temporal Lags, and Site Considerations
info: Desert hydrology and irrigation create volatile weekly mosquito metrics. This session demonstrates how routine trapping, PRISM weather data, and micro-habitat surveys can be integrated into situational awareness tiers, targeted routing, and equity-minded performance checks— all of which are portable to regions with seasonal or intermittent WNV activity.

title: See Data Differently: How Data Visualization is Elevating Environmental Health
info: When environmental threats strike, every second counts. Discover how data visualization is transforming how environmental public health professionals detect pollution hotspots, track disease outbreaks, and communicate critical risks to communities. Learn practical strategies to modernize your data systems, break down information silos, and turn overwhelming datasets into clear, life-saving insights. Join us to explore real-world case studies and interactive tools that are reshaping environmental health protection in the digital age. Ready to see your data differently?

title: Training Evolution: Advancing Skills Beyond the Inspection
info: Training 2.0 is where foundational knowledge meets real-world readiness. Designed for newly authorized REHS staff in Mecklenburg County, this second-tier program blends in-classroom instruction with field application to build confidence, consistency, and independence. From complaint investigations to permitting and enforcement, Training 2.0 takes a holistic approach to professional development, equipping staff with the tools, context, and judgment needed to manage their territories independently and effectively. It’s not just about knowing the job—it’s about owning it.

title: Whole House Model to Equitably Address Home Conditions
info: The Whole House Model: Creating pathways to sustainable investment for integrated housing intervention programs that improve health and economic outcomes in disinvested communities.

title: PFAS & Pesticides - An Underappreciated Source of Contamination?
info: In the Midwestern corn belt, the concentration and scale of row crop agriculture necessitates the use of large quantities and combinations of agrochemicals, including insecticides, fungicides, and herbicides. New research has highlighted the important relationship between PFAS chemicals (also known as "forever chemicals") and pesticides -- including PFAS as active ingredients in many pesticide products. With the EPA's 2025 approval of four new PFAS containing pesticides, exploration of this topic is more relevant than ever. This introductory session will give an high-level overview of PFAS in agriculture through chemical application, the ecological and human health concerns of exposure, and invite discussion on research priorities and interventions on the topic.

title: Closing Keynote Address
info: An Overview of the Gulf South Vector Educational Center for Training, Outreach, and Resources (VECTOR) Collaborative: Strengthening Programmatic Vector Control Capacity
